issue_title: Making timeout_after/ignore_after truly composable
issue_body:
[As you know](https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/issues/46#issuecomment-216083910) I'm a big fan on `timeout_after`. The awesome thing about it is that it's composable, in the sense that I can treat any random coroutine as a black box and slap a `timeout_after` around it to impose a timeout on it, so long as the called coroutine follows some simple rules. Basically, it should be written so that my timeout fires, then it promptly cleans up and exits. Not an onerous constraint.

Except... it's hard to follow this rule in coroutines that themselves want to use `timeout_after`. The docs allude to some of this; here's an example where everything goes wrong because the internal coroutine can't tell whether it's the internal timeout that fired, or the outer one:

``` python
  async def get_first_responding(urls):
      for url in urls:
          try:
              # per-URL timeout
              async with timeout_after(5):
                  return (await curio_http.get(url)).text
          except TaskTimeout:
              print("Too slow! Trying the next")
      raise RuntimeError("everyone's too slow!")

  async def main():
      # Global timeout:
      async with timeout_after(10):
          print(await get_first_responding([...]))
```

I suggest that the key missing feature is that when we see a timeout exception, we need to be able to figure out whether it's one that means that _we_ have timed out and should clean up and let our caller get on with things, versus one that means _a thing we called_ has timed out and we should recover however makes sense (e.g. by falling back to trying another server). Are we inside the `timeout_after` that fired, or outside?

```
 outer code       <--- Are we here?
   timeout_after
     inner code       <-- or here?
```

Proposal: arrange for the inner code to see a different exception type than the outer code.

Implementation: give each `timeout_after` a magic cookie, and when a timeout fires, inject an instance of the "inner" exception type with the magic cookie attached. When an exception passes through a `timeout_after`, check if the cookies match, and if so, convert to the "outer" exception type. (And `ignore_after` should check the cookie, and swallow the exception iff it matches.)

When there are stacked timeouts that fire simultaneously, e.g.

``` python
  async with timeout_after(5):
      # -- mark --
      async with timeout_after(10):
          ...
```

then it should always be the outermost timeout whose cookie is used (so the line at `-- mark --` should see an "inner" exception, not an "outer" one).

Unsolved problem: I am drawing a blank on what to call these two exception types to distinguish them :-(. OperationTimeOut versus CancelledTimeOut? [Edit: or maybe ExternalTimeout / InternalTimeout? CallerTimeout / CalleeTimeout? OutsideTimeout / InsideTimeout? LocalTimeout?]

(Speaking of timeout exception names, why is it called `TaskTimeout`? It doesn't really have anything to do with Tasks in particular, does it? I mean, any more so than literally everything else in curio?)

issue_title: Let users specify rounds
issue_body: Currently, rounds is calculated by ``rounds = 6 + 52 / n``, where n is the length of the array of 32-bit integers converted from the input bytes.

issue_title: encrypt_hex should be encrypt, decrypt_hex should be decryt
issue_body:
With the C-based version of XXTEA, the methods "encrypt" and "decrypt" were replaced with "encrypt_hex" and "decrypt_hex", respectively. However, concerning future development, it would probably make a lot sense not to introduce new method names for the default behavior.

I'd suggest to rename the current "encrypt" into "encrypt_raw", and "encrypt_hex" back into "encrypt". The same with decrypt. This also maintains backwards compatibility and consistency concerning method names.

issue_title: How should 'echo' be defined in Makefiles to ensure that it supports 'echo -n'?
issue_body:
Running pyang tests on a Mac I find that `echo -n` outputs a newline and a literal `-n`, i.e. echo doesn't support this option.

As I understand it, (a) `make` (GNU make) will always use `/bin/sh` and (b) `/bin/sh`'s  built-in `echo` doesn't support `-n`. So I wouldn't expect `echo -n` to work correctly, yet it's used in many of the `Makefiles`.

I wonder whether I have something wrong in my environment. Does this work as expected for others on Macs?

issue_title: drectangle with the size < 1
issue_body:
I was trying to use drectangle with some normalized coordinate system, where x/y are in range 0..1. This allows me to make processing independent from image resolution, because modern CCTV cameras have multiple output video streams of different resolution

Current drectangle contract says that its size can't be less than 1:
```C++
 drectangle centered_drect (
        const vector<double,2>& p,
        double width,
        double height
    );
    /*!
        ensures
            - returns a rectangle R such that:
                - center(R) == p
                - if (width < 1 || height < 1)
                    - R.width() == 0 
                    - R.height() == 0 
                    - R.is_empty() == true
                - else
                    - R.width() == width
                    - R.height() == height 
    !*/
```

Is there any way to change this? It would be nice to have rectangle primitive without such strong restriction and inventing the wheel with some new rectangle class does not look interesting

The main problem I see are right() and bottom() values. Now they are pointing to a positions *inside* rectangle. OpenCV's implementation makes simple : right = left + width, while dlib's: right = left + width - 1

I can imagine some other situations where this can be usefull:
1. DNN can produce drectangles (YOLO, SSD for example are working this way)
2. some affine-transform of drectangle

issue_title: Drifting weights
issue_body:
I'd like to backtest a monthly rebalance strategy with a custom WeighTarget class that:
1) sets target.temp['weights'] (the ones to rebalance "to")
2) calculates drifted weights (the ones to rebalance "from")
What's the best way to implement it? Somehow I need to trigger a SecurityBase's update, right?

Thanks,
r.

Here below a simplified version of my WeighTarget class:

class WeighTargetAfterDrift(bt.Algo):
    def __init__(self, target_weights, prices):
        self.tw = target_weights
        self.prices = prices

        self.time_before = target_weights.index[0]
        self.current_weights = target_weights.loc[self.time_before]
        self.prices_before = self.prices.ix[self.time_before]

    def __call__(self, target):
        if target.now in self.tw.index:
            self.time_after = target.now
            self.prices_after = self.prices.ix[self.time_after]

            bench_rets = self.prices_after / self.prices_before - 1.
            drifted_w = _make_weights_drift(self.current_weights, bench_rets)

            target.temp['weights'] = self.tw.loc[self.time_after]

            # for the next __call__
            self.time_before = self.time_after
            self.prices_before = self.prices_after
            self.current_weights = self.tw.loc[self.time_after]

        return True

issue_title: Adding requests for merge support
issue_body:
Add the `request` subcommand that enables handling of requests:

```
git-repo [--path=<path>] [-v -v...] <target> request [<user>/<repo>] list
git-repo [--path=<path>] [-v -v...] <target> request [<user>/<repo>] fetch <request>
```

that makes it possible to list all requests, and fetch a request thanks to its `id`.

issue_body:

issue_title: Check that docstrings are triple-double-quotes
issue_body:
There are many guidelines proposed within the [PEP257](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0257/) document. One of the easiest ones to check is that docstrings should always use triple-double-quotes """.

I don't know if this is out of scope for this module (since it's names `autopep8`), but I think a lot of the guidelines proposed in PEP257 would be nice additions to this program.

issue_title: With Python 3, the body of POST requests is base64-encoded
issue_body:
As a consequence of commit 09b7ccf (more precisely, of the conversion in the request body setter), the body of the POST requests are unreadable in the tape file:
```patch
--- tape.yaml
+++ tape.yaml
@@ -41,8 +41,7 @@ interactions:
       content-type: [application/json; charset=utf-8]
     status: {code: 200, message: OK}
 - request:
-    body: mail_host=example.com
+    body: !!binary |
+      bWFpbF9ob3N0PWV4YW1wbGUuY29t
     headers:
       accept-encoding: ['gzip, deflate']
       content-type: [application/x-www-form-urlencoded]
```

It makes it much harder to diff changes between tapes committed in a VCS, and to identify what each request is coming from in the code.

```

Note that the exception is **NOT** caught by `except Exception as error:` in `task_done`, which makes it impossible to handle.

This issue seems to be known / common:
 * https://bugs.python.org/issue17560

And it also seems to be an anti-pattern to send tons of data to / from the subprocess. My goal here is to report this in order to get the conversation started, ultimately I would like this exception to be handled by `pebble` in such a way that I can then handle it in my code.

This issue might be related with https://github.com/noxdafox/pebble/issues/28

issue_title: no channel.CFormat when reading mdf4
issue_body:
Hi, I am trying to read a sorted mdf4.1 with mdfreader version master0.2.4 from GITHub.
The following error is thrown:

>   File "C:\Anaconda2\lib\site-packages\mdfreader\mdf4reader.py", line 1128, in readSortedRecord
>   rec[channel.name][r] = channel.CFormat.unpack(buf[channel.posByteBeg:channel.posByteEnd])[0]
>   AttributeError: channel instance has no attribute 'CFormat'

I tracked that the error is caused, when it comes to read the channel 'ms', which is added to the channelList from the record.loadInfo() function.
Looking in the debugging mode into the channel objects, CFormat really does not exist for channel 'ms'. It exists only Format, which is however an empty string. This struct, CFormat, should be created in  channel.set() from channel.Format which has been set by datatypeformat4(). However, channel.set is never called for the added channel 'ms', so that Format remains empty and CFormat is never created.  
Channel.set() is called in line 990 in fct loadInfo() of class record and before channel ms is added.
For channel 'ms' (and 'hour, 'day', etc) the function channel.setCANOpen() is called. In this function Format is not filled by datatypeformat4() and CFormat is not created.
I added the following lines of code, which I copied from channel.set(), to the end of function channel.setCanopen():
`        if self.signalDataType not in (13, 14):  # processed by several channels
            if not self.channelType == 1:  # if not VSLD
                self.Format = datatypeformat4(self.signalDataType, self.bitCount)
            else:  # VLSD
                self.Format = datatypeformat4(0, self.bitCount)
        self.CFormat = Struct(self.Format)
`
The script then runs without throwing this error, but I still need to check if data Import works correctly ...

issue_title: when calling append on a Path object, split on '/'
Code for notification?
issue_body:
Hi to everyone,

I need help to perform a simple task.

I'm using a BLE Nano to read from an analog pin and then sending via notification the reading. What I want to do is to develop a application that can show on screen the value continuously (I manually change the value using a potentiometer, so when the resistance is maximum the value shown will be e.g 750, when resistance is minimum the value shown will be e.g 7).
My BLE device has two characteristics: 
- one for writing (handle 14)
- one for notify (handle 16)

Using a Android app developed by RedBearLab I can receive data from the analog pin, so the sketch works. Now I have to receive this data on the computer.

To turn ON the notification I need to write "aa"  (0x61,0x61) on characteristic 14, up to now there are no problems. Once the notification is ON I do not receive nothing. BUT if I add the READ property on characteristic 16 I can read the value on it. What I'm trying to say is that after a enter 'aa' on characteristic 14 I can read the value on characteristic 16, but I prefer to receive this data continuously via notification. 

I understand this is simple task, but this is my very first time I work with a BLE device and Python programming. I hope someone could help me.

Thank you.

This is the code I wrote, please let me know what I need to do to achieve my goal.

```
import struct
from bluepy.btle import *


class MyDelegate(DefaultDelegate):
    def __init__(self):
        DefaultDelegate.__init__(self)

    def handleNotification(self, cHandle, data):
        print(data)

per = Peripheral("ea:c7:bd:19:0a:83","random")
per.setDelegate(MyDelegate())

print("Connected")

try:
    per.writeCharacteristic(14,bytes('aa','utf-8'))
    print("writing done")
    while True:
        if per.waitForNotifications(1.0):
            print("Notification")
            continue
    print("Waiting")
finally:
    per.disconnect()
    print ("Disconnected")
```

```Python
import jedi

source = '''\
"""this file encapsulates the bibtex handling routines.
Only in a separate file because elpy crashed on bibtexparser import :-(
"""

import bibtexparser
import re
import os

def processBibtex(docname, bibtex):
    """process a raw bibtex input as downloaded from wiki.

    Main steps:
    - remove any wiki anchors
    - sanitize via bibtex library
    - write out bibtex file
    - return a list of bibtex keys, necessary for postprocessing latex

    input:
    - docname: document and subdirectory to be processed
    - bibtex: string containing the collated files

    output:
    - list of bibtex keys
    """

    # remove any wiki anchors, as defined in
    # https://meta.wikimedia.org/wiki/Help:Anchors

    bibtex = re.sub('<( *)div(.*?)>', '', bibtex, re.S)
    bibtex = re.sub('<( *)/( *)div(.*?)>', '', bibtex, re.S)

    # sanitize via bibtex library
    parser = bibtexparser.bparser.BibTexParser()
    parser.customization = bibtexparser.customization.homogeneize_latex_encoding
    bibDB = bibtexparser.loads(bibtex,
                               parser=parser)

    # lowercase all the bibtex keys, just because that's the way pandoc inserts the refs:
    for e in bibDB.e
    
    # dump it to file
    writer = bibtexparser.bwriter.BibTexWriter()
    with open (os.path.join(docname, 'tex', 'bib.bib'),
               'w') as bh:
        bh.write(writer.write(bibDB))

    # get a list keys to return:
    keys = [x['ID'] for x in bibDB.entries]
    print "bibtexkeys (1): ", keys
    return keys
'''

script = jedi.Script(column=20, source=source, line=39, encoding='utf-8', path=u'/Users/hkarl/c.prog/mw2pdf/bibtexHandler.py')
script.completions()
```

I have a problem with a python process all of a sudden taking up all 24GB of RAM. That only happens when i point the autocompletion to the location where the stubs from the Autodesk Maya devkit are (containing pyMel, PySide2 and OpenMaya). In total these stub files are 60MB.
I've tried this with SublimeText/Anaconda and Atom/autocomplete-python. Both use Jedi.

```
import pymel.core as pm
pm.

I have the same problem on my desk PC as well as my Laptop

issue_title: reading cursor.return_value breaks fetches
issue_body:
User calls stored procedure with callproc.
Stored procedure returns resultset and return value.
If return value is read before resultset, resultsset is always empty.
It's not clear to me if it can be fixed easily. I suppose it should be at least asserted.

```python
import sys
import pytds

assert len(sys.argv) == 5, 'Provide 4 db connection arguments'

with pytds.connect(*sys.argv[1:]) as connection:
    with connection.cursor() as cursor:
        cursor.execute("""
create proc TestProcedure
as
begin
    select 1 union select 2 union select 3
    select 'a' union select 'b' union select 'c'
    return 9
end
""")
        cursor.callproc('TestProcedure')

        # HERE:        
        # print cursor.return_value # if uncommented, breaks fetchall
        
        print cursor.fetchall()   # returns 1,2,3
        cursor.nextset()
        print cursor.fetchall()   # returns a,b,c
        print cursor.return_value # returns 9
        
    connection.rollback()
```

issue_title: GDFS thanks Fredrik Orre for his donation.
The typedefs and other definitions from fake_libc_include conflicts with the actual defines (parsing sqlite.c).
issue_body:
I am trying to parse sqlite.c (amalgamated), and reprint the AST using the `examples/c-to-c.py`. My environment is `Ubuntu 15.04` with `gcc 4.9.2`.  I am using `sqlite 3.13`. My makefile is below.

```
sqlite.c: c/sqlite3.c c/sqlite3.h utils/fake_libc_include/_fake_defines.h
	gcc -nostdinc -D'__attribute__(x)=' -I utils/fake_libc_include/ -E c/sqlite3.c -Ic/ > sqlite.c_
	mv sqlite.c_ sqlite.c

s.c: sqlite.c
	python3 ./examples/c-to-c.py sqlite.c > s.c_
	mv s.c_ s.c

s: s.c
	gcc s.c c/shell.c -o s
```

On running the `gcc s.c` command, it fails with

```
s.c:3:13: error: conflicting types for ‘__builtin_va_list’
 typedef int __builtin_va_list;

s.c:2816:22: note: expected ‘struct __va_list_tag *’ but argument is of type ‘int’
s.c:2843:25: error: expected ‘,’ before ‘)’ token
     *__builtin_va_arg(ap) = val;
                         ^

```
and
```
s.c:3192:5: error: dereferencing pointer to incomplete type
     *pTm = *pX;
     ^
s.c:3192:12: error: dereferencing pointer to incomplete type
     *pTm = *pX;
            ^
s.c: In function ‘localtimeOffset’:
s.c:3204:13: error: storage size of ‘sLocal’ isn’t known
   struct tm sLocal;
             ^
```

I assumed from the `Changelog` that I would be able to parse and reprint the AST of `sqlite.c`. Is it supported?

issue_body: KafkaClient should disconnect sockets that have not been active for the configured max idle duration.

issue_title: Support FetchRequest v4 / Message Format v2
issue_body:
```
In Kafka 0.11, the structure of the 'MessageSet' and 'Message' were significantly changed. Not only were new fields added to support new features like exactly once semantics and record headers, but the recursive nature of the previous versions of the message format was eliminated in favor of a flat structure. A 'MessageSet' is now called a 'RecordBatch', which contains one or more 'Records' (and not 'Messages'). When compression is enabled, the RecordBatch header remains uncompressed, but the Records are compressed together. Further, multiple fields in the 'Record' are varint encoded, which leads to significant space savings for larger batches. 
```

See https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/A+Guide+To+The+Kafka+Protocol#AGuideToTheKafkaProtocol-Messagesets

issue_title: Possible typo in example JSON-serializing KafkaProducer
issue_body:
I was trying out your library today and noticed that the following example from the README uses ```json.loads``` as the serializer instead of ```json.dumps``` as I'd expect.

```
>>> # Serialize json messages
>>> import json
>>> producer = KafkaProducer(value_serializer=json.loads)
>>> producer.send('fizzbuzz', {'foo': 'bar'})
```
While trying it out, I noticed that using ```json.loads``` as in the original example results in nothing appearing on my active consumers, but replacing it with ```json.dumps``` causes the consumers to display the messages.

issue_title: peTimeDateStamp and /Brepro flag
issue_body:
Hi,

Just for information: if a pe.DIRECTORY_ENTRY_DEBUG struct has a Type of 0x10, then windbg preview considers pe.TimeDateStamp as a build ID, not a valid timestamp. VS2013 with /Brepro just sets the TimeDateStamp to 0xFFFFFFFF and does not add the entry, but MS pe files have it (if you change an existing entry type to 0x10 of a regular pe file, then windbg preview adds the warning).

I dont really know how (and if) you can render this information in pefile, but it may be useful for malware analysts/tools : build IDs may in some cases seem "valid" and I did not found any other way to differentiate them.

Cheers

issue_title: Refresh on new session fails
issue_body:
```python
Python 3.6.0 (default, Mar  4 2017, 12:32:37) 
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 8.0.0 (clang-800.0.42.1)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import rom
>>> rom.session.refresh_all()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/.../ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/rom/util.py", line 592, in refresh_all
    self.refresh(*self.known.values(), force=kwargs.get('force'))
AttributeError: 'Session' object has no attribute 'known'
>>> 
```

I think there are some other code paths that will hit a missing `known` attribute as well.

issue_title: Allow rendering from stdin without using a -
issue_body: Before 0.7, I was able to pipe a dot file to xdot to render it by running `something | xdot`. Now, I have to run `something | xdot -`. This isn't really a problem per-say, it's just a bit inconsistent with other unix commands where the `-` is optional if unambiguous.

issue_title: collate chars w/ slight doctop variations
issue_body:
Collate_chars' [sort](https://github.com/jsvine/pdfplumber/blob/master/pdfplumber/utils.py#L43) assumes that colinear words have the same exact doctop. In the wild, there's sometimes variations on this--especially for fillable pdfs (at least I think that's what I'm seeing).  I hacked around this with words by mucking with precision, but the variations here are much bigger--1 or 2 pixels. 

I'm hacking around this with an optional snap_to_y_grid arg, and something like: 

    if snap_to_y_grid:
        snap_to_grid = lambda x: round(x/float(snap_to_y_grid))
        chars["doctop"] = chars["doctop"].apply(snap_to_grid)

This alters the underlying dataframe values (maybe?)--which I assume would require a copy or something, or setting a different value. Any thoughts?

issue_title: Remove ITargetConnection.recv max_bytes parameter
issue_body: `ITargetConnection.recv` has a `max_bytes` parameter that is mostly an annoyance. In existing code, it has been set to some arbitrarily high number. We should eliminate the parameter, give it a helpful default, or something.

issue_title: SSL/TLS certificates are not validated!
issue_body:
Hi Folks,

from my Impression current imbox does **not** validate SSL/TLS certificates.

https://github.com/martinrusev/imbox/blob/master/imbox/imap.py#L21

calls `IMAP4_SSL` default constructor which looks like:

```
imaplib.IMAP4_SSL(host='', port=IMAP4_SSL_PORT, keyfile=None, certfile=None, ssl_context=None)
```

As you can see at `ssl_context=None`. This default configuration does not validate SSL/TLS certificates!

**Using the default configuration makes software using Imbox vulnerable!**

Idea to solve this problem (untested sketch code!)

```
import ssl as pythonssllib

       if ssl:
            context = pythonssllib.create_default_context()
            if not self.port:
                self.port = 993
            self.server = self.IMAP4_SSL(self.hostname, self.port, ssl_context=context)
        else:
            if not self.port:
                self.port = 143
            self.server = self.IMAP4(self.hostname, self.port)     
```

Issuetrace: https://github.com/certtools/intelmq/issues/532

issue_title: Security: remote code execution
issue_body:
Hi,

I am currently auditing many open-source reddit clients and have
found an important vulnerability in rtv involving massive remote code
execution.

The problem
===========

In rtv/terminal.py:471 (rev. 20b59d5), in open_browser(...) the
command variable is built unsafely. An url such as:

    http://test.com/?q=');print('hacked');print('

would allow python code injection which would lately be happily
executed by python through the Popen call.

Attack scenario
===============

An attacker could craft a malicious url executing code (most likely
downloading a trojan) and post it under an attractive reddit title.
Anyone trying to open it in the webbrowser through rtv would get
hacked.

Also note that in my example a tab is still opened to the url

    http://test.com/?q=

so one could very well provide a real url in order to mask his
attack for those that do not use rtv.

It can also be mixed with other attacks as I have found that most
other terminal reddit clients have the same kind of vulnerability
(although bash injection is more common than python). That raises the
criticity of the problem as it means that an attack on reddit clients
is likely to be very successful: it's not just rtv.

The impact
==========

The impact is critical: hundreds of computers can be impacted at
once. While it needs user interaction it is very easy to hide the
attack from one.

Recommended fix
===============

The best would be not to evaluate python code at all. Every code
injection problem comes from bugs at the interface between two
languages or layers.

The quickest solution though would be to escape quotes and
backslashes within the url. Escaping each and every character would
break lots of urls but at the very least quotes and backslashes
shouldn't be part of it.

I don't recommend the quick solution though: executing external code
through proper means is better.

Course of action
================

In my humble opinion your best course of action is to patch it as
soon as possible then issue a clear warning to already existing
users, maybe by getting a CVE number (Common Vulnerability and
Exposures, a global reference for all security vulnerabilities in
software of some importance).

I hope this finds you well,

Sincerely,

Cédric Picard

issue_title: feature request: halo ellipticity estimators
issue_body:
Similar to running an NG (tangential shear) correlation, but the user passes in an extra parameter, call it phi, for each object in the lens catalog. Treecorr returns the result of two optimally weighted estimators of the elliptical lensing shear, and two corresponding cross-components. All four results are arrays of length N, where N is the number of bins in angle (or perhaps projected scale in Mpc/h).

This extra input parameter phi is used to rotate the shear field for each lens into a common Cartesian coordinate system (gamma_1 and gamma_2 instead of gamma_+ and gamma_x). Having performed this rotation, the desired estimators to calculate are
gamma_4theta = Sum_i (w_i * gamma_1i * cos(4theta) + w_i * gamma_2i * sin(4theta)) / Sum_i (w_i)
gamma_const = Sum_i (w_i * gamma_1i) / Sum_i (w_i)

gamma_4theta_x = Sum_i (w_i * gamma_1i * cos(4theta) - w_i * gamma_2i * sin(4theta)) / Sum_i (w_i)
gamma_const_x = Sum_i (w_i * gamma_2i) / Sum_i (w_i)

where the index i runs over all lens-source pairs, and w_i is the usual weight for gamma (for example, sum of squares of measurement error and shape noise).

issue_title: editable_selectAll and (sorry) IE8
Configuration getter
issue_body:
One of the new ways in Version 1 to create settings is with the `setter` function on the `Configuration` class.

```python
from sanic_jwt import Configuration

class MyConfiguration(Configuration):
    def set_access_token_name(self):
        return 'jwt'

Initialize(
    app,
    configuration_class=MyConfiguration)
```

Then, the `Initialization` class goes and installs all the various config settings on a `config` object.

```
>>> print(config.access_token_name)
'jwt'
```

I propose that we change the way that `config` is used throughout the backend to make it a callable. Instead of doing `config.access_token_name` it would become `config.get('access_token_name')`.

Why?

Then, we can not only have dynamic attributes set at initialization, but also when called.

```python
class MyConfiguration(Configuration):
    async def get_access_token_name(self):
        return await some_fancy_function()
```

issue_title: pyodbc and pylint static analysis
issue_body:
When I run my code through the static analysis tool [pylint,](https://www.pylint.org/) I get the following error for all calls to pyodbc.connect():

`Module 'pyodbc' has no 'connect' member`

I get similar errors for pyodbc exception objects.  It appears pylint is not able to ascertain the functions and attributes within the pyodbc module. I don't know how this can fixed (because pyodbc is almost entirely C++), but if it can, it would be kinda handy.

FYI, the workaround I have at the moment is to tell pylint to ignore the pyodbc module entirely, by adding the following line to my pylint config file:

```
[TYPECHECK]
ignored-modules=pyodbc
```

issue_title: UnicodeDecodeError on table value functions with non ASCII column names
issue_body:
This might be related to the general issue #112, but as it's a very specific case with exampel code and infrastrcuture details, I decided to open a separate issue:

When running an SQL query that accesses a table value function (TVF) with non ASCII characters in the column name (e.g. `ä`), pyodbc fails with:

> UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0xe4 in position 1: invalid continuation byte

To reproduce this, first create a trivial table value function TVF_temp that returns a single value with the heading "Waehrung" (ASCIIfied German for "currency"):

```sql
use SomeDatabase;

if object_id('SomeDatabase.dbo.TVF_temp') is not null
    drop function dbo.TVF_temp;
go

create function dbo.TVF_temp(@Waehrung char(1)) returns table as return
select @Waehrung as [Waehrung];
go
```
Using SQL Server Management Studio, it is possible to query this function:
```sql
select * from dbo.TVF_temp('€');
```
As expected, the result is:
```
Waehrung
--------
€

(1 row(s) affected)
```
Now run the same query using Python and pyodbc (replace `SomeServer` and `SomeDatabase` by approriate values and possibly add additonal connection parameters):

```python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
import logging
import pyodbc
from contextlib import closing

# Specify connection.
server = 'SomeServer'
database = 'SomeDatabase'
connection_string = 'DRIVER={{SQL Server}};SERVER={server};DATABASE={database}'.format(server=server, database=database)
query_statement = "select * from dbo.TVF_temp('€')"

# Setup logging.
_log = logging.getLogger('temp')
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)

_log.info('connecting to %s', connection_string)
with pyodbc.connect(connection_string) as connection:
    with closing(connection.cursor()) as cursor:
        _log.info
        try:
            for row in cursor.execute(query_statement):
                _log.info('row=%s', row)
        except UnicodeDecodeError as error:
            _log.error('error.args=%s', error.args)
            raise
_log.info('done')
```
With Python 3.5, the output is as expected:
```
INFO:temp:connecting to DRIVER={SQL Server};SERVER=...;DATABASE=...
INFO:temp:row = ('€', )
INFO:temp:done
```
This is exactly what was desired, so passing data and retrieving results with Unicode characters works fine.

Now change the column name `[Waehrung]` to `[Währung]`:
```sql
create function dbo.TVF_temp(@Waehrung char(1)) returns table as return
select @Waehrung as [Währung];
go
```
In SQL Server Management Studio this still yields the expected result, with a nice "ä" in the Heading:
```
Währung
-------
€

(1 row(s) affected)
```
Running the Python code again though fails with:
```
INFO:temp:connecting to DRIVER={SQL Server};SERVER=...;DATABASE=...
ERROR:temp:error.args=('utf-8', b'W\xe4hrung', 1, 2, 'invalid continuation byte')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\Temp\pyodbc_unicode_error.py", line 23, in <module>
    for row in cursor.execute(query_statement):
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0xe4 in position 1: invalid continuation byte
In error.args you can see that "Währung" is encoded using CP1252 (b'W\xe4hrung'), nevertheless pyodbc attempts to decode it as UTF-8.
```

Adding CHARSET=UTF-8 to the connection string does not change this.

When researching the topic, I found the following ODBC Unicode tutorial: ​https://www.progress.com/odbc/resources/tutorials/unicode/non-unicode. According to this it seems that on Unix ODBC indeeed uses UTF-8 while under Windows it's either CP1252 or UTF16/UCS2.

Interestingly, running the above code with Python 2.6 yields the following result:
```
INFO:temp:connecting to DRIVER={SQL Server};SERVER=...;DATABASE=...
INFO:temp:row = ('\x80', )
INFO:temp:done
```
So it works, but Python just uses a byte string to represent the result, effectively leaving the CP1252 Euro Sign at 0x80 instead of translating it to the Unicode code point 0x20ac. I only added this example for comparison, I don't expect any fixes or changes for the dated pyodbc version we were using with Python 2.

So seemingly proper Unicode translation happens for queries sent, parameters specified and results transmitted over ODBC. For indirectly quering columns names used by table value functions, no translation takes placed and things are left to the mercy of Python default encoding. Which in case of Python 3's default result in a UnicodeDecodeError when attempting to decode CP1252 using UTF-8.

Here are the details on the infrastructure used:

Python
* Python 3.5.1 (via Anaconda 3), pyodbc 3.0.10 
* Python 2.6.6, pyodbc 2.1.11 

MS SQL Server properties: 
* Version: 11.0.56133.0 
* Language: English 
* Server collation: Latin_1_General_CI_AS 
* Operating System: Windows NT 6.1 (7601) 
* Platform: NT x64 

ODBC Driver 1: 
* Name: SQL Server 
* Version: 6.01.7601.17514 
* File: SQLSRV32.DDL 

ODBC Driver 2 (same result): 
* SQL Server Native Client 11.0 
* Version: 2011.110.3000.00 
* SQLNCLI11.DDL

Add feature to support SASL PLAIN auth options for LDAP
issue_body:
Feature request to add auth options to support the Enterprise auth SASL methods, which include PLAIN (LDAP).
 
### Expected behavior
There would be a minimal set of server parameters required to configure [LDAP](https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/core/security-ldap-external/).

The mandatory parameters would include:

`--ldapServers`, `--ldapQueryUser` and `--ldapQueryPassword`

The optional parameters would include:

`--ldapTransportSecurity`, `--ldapUserToDNMapping` and `--ldapAuthzQueryTemplate`

The remaining LDAP options are for very niche configurations and probably not required for most generalised use cases.

### Actual/current behavior
Currently the auth feature only supports internal auth methods, such as SCRAM-SHA1.

In order to support LDAP, the initial user/role will need for be captured as a _mlaunch_ parameter and populated in `$external` accordingly.

### Environment

| Software         | Version
| ---------------- | -------
| mtools           | 1.3.1
| MongoDB server   | 3.6.x 
| Operating system | OSX/Linux

issue_title: battery_level.py recommended changes
issue_body:
Module battery_level.py lines 343 and 350 raise an `AttributeError ` when the Py3status class is instantiated and the battery is below either threshold. 
The `self.last_known_status` variable isn't declared yet so the threshold conditionals fail. Otherwise if the battery starts above the threshold and drops below it, the error is avoided.
Declaring `last_known_status = ''` at the top of Py3status fixes the problem.


A `subprocess.call()` to notify-send on lines 159 and 364 of battery_level.py raises a `FileNotFoundError` (python3.5) when libnotify is not installed. Neither libnotify nor dunst is listed in the module requirements.
The module should probably use the py3status `notify_user()` method so people can utilize the -b flag, and a note in --help mentioning the libnotify requirement along with dunst might be helpful.

Attached is a patch for battery_level.py to fix these issues.

(sorry about the extension, github doesn't allow .patch files apparently)
[battery_level.txt](https://github.com/ultrabug/py3status/files/505486/battery_level.txt)

issue_title: idea: method to load jsonlines file to list
issue_body:
Hi,

Love the library. We're starting to use jsonlines as our de-facto format for storing datasets for data science, since it's useful for streaming and batch processing. What this means is that all the json objects in a given file are homogeneous, and we almost always want to load the objects into a list in Python. 

We've currently got our own (super simple) function to do this, but I imagine this is quite a common use-case so would be good for the library to have something like a `Reader().to_list()` function. 

What do you think? Happy to implement this and make a PR if you think it's a good idea.

issue_title: Calculated Services Supported
issue_body:
Most of the sample applications, and real ones, have code like this:
```
    # get the services supported
    services_supported = this_application.get_services_supported()
    if _debug: _log.debug("    - services_supported: %r", services_supported)

    this_device.protocolServicesSupported = services_supported.value

issue_title: Expose handlers as classes/interfaces: ClientHandler, DisplayHandler, LifespanHandler, etc.
issue_body:
When user's code creates MyClientHandler it can use these interfaces to check if his callbacks signatures are declared properly - to look for typos or deprecated callbacks removed from upstream CEF. Also would be useful for auto completion.

Python has the concept of abc - Abstract Bases Classes - see:
* abc module - https://docs.python.org/2/library/abc.html
* PEP 3119 - https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3119/
* http://stackoverflow.com/questions/372042/

Example:
```
class ClientHandler(metaclass=ABCMeta):
    __subclass__ = {DisplayHandler, LifespanHandler, ...}

class MyClientHandler:
    def __init__(self):
        assert issubclass(MyClientHandler, ClientHandler)
```

The example above probably requires implementing all callbacks - we only need to validate functions that have been declared. Whether such callback name is valid and whether its number of parameters are valid.

Also do this for other non-handler classes, but which also act as callbacks:
* StringVisitor
* CookieVisitor
* WebRequestClient

issue_title: immobilus is incompatible with logging.Formatter()
issue_body:
Hi @pokidovea! Just found one issue of using immobilius fake functions as class attributes, in particular in the logging.Formatter class.

## Issue

The stdlib formatter contains following code

```python
class Formatter(object):
    converter = time.localtime
```

The converter is called to convert log record timestamp to ASCII string. The problem is, `time.localtime` (which has type "builtin_function_or_method") and `fake_localtime` (which has type "function") behave differently as class attributes: the latter is bound with a parent class instance on initialization.

## Case without patching

```python
# Python 3.6.2 (default, Aug  2 2017, 15:33:18)
>>> import logging
>>> logging.Formatter('%(asctime)s').format(logging.makeLogRecord({}))
'2018-06-22 18:19:20,435'
>>> logging.Formatter.converter
<built-in function localtime>
>>> logging.Formatter().converter
<built-in function localtime>
```

## Case with patching

```python
# Python 3.6.2 (default, Aug  2 2017, 15:33:18)
>>> from immobilus import immobilus
>>> import logging
>>> logging.Formatter('%(asctime)s').format(logging.makeLogRecord({}))
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/roman/.pyenv/versions/3.6.2/lib/python3.6/logging/__init__.py", line 577, in format
    record.asctime = self.formatTime(record, self.datefmt)
  File "/Users/roman/.pyenv/versions/3.6.2/lib/python3.6/logging/__init__.py", line 513, in formatTime
    ct = self.converter(record.created)
TypeError: fake_localtime() takes from 0 to 1 positional arguments but 2 were given
>>> logging.Formatter.converter
<function fake_localtime at 0x10ebe3950>
>>> logging.Formatter().converter
<bound method fake_localtime of <logging.Formatter object at 0x10e9ccac8>>
```

## Workaround

For myself I found a workaround which kind of works, but solves only this specific case

```python
>>> from immobilus import immobilus
>>> import logging
>>> logging.Formatter.converter = staticmethod(logging.Formatter.converter)
>>> logging.Formatter('%(asctime)s').format(logging.makeLogRecord({}))
'2018-06-22 18:20:46,534'
```

## Proper solution

Probably, the only generalized solution is to turn fake_localtime to an object with a `__get__` method, always returning itself (disallowing binding), but I haven't experimented with this.

issue_title: flatten / flat_map ?
issue_body:
```python
import asyncio
from aiostream import stream


async def g1():
    for i in range(10):
        yield 2 * i
        await asyncio.sleep(.3)


async def g2(n):
    yield n
    await asyncio.sleep(.1)
    yield n + 1


async def main():
    # Create stream of numbers
    xs = stream.iterate(g1())
    # Map each number to a new stream
    ys = stream.map(xs, lambda n: stream.iterate(g2(n)))
    # Flatten a stream of streams into a stream of numbers
    zs = stream.flatten(ys)

    # Or just
    # zs = stream.flat_map(xs, lambda n: stream.iterate(g2(n)))

    await stream.list(zs)
    # Expecting [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, ..., 19]


if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(main())
    loop.close()
```

`flat_map` is quite a handy thing in reactive world. Is it possible to implement it in `aiostream`?

It should work like a `stream.merge`, but `merge` expects all streams given as arguments prior to call, but in the case above streams arrive asynchronly.

Also, `switch_map` should be easy implement after `flat_map`.

issue_title: Order of HTML attributes in a content of feed entry is unstable
issue_body:
#### Problem description *feedparser* does not preserve and does not determine an order of HTML attributes in a *content* of feed *entry*. So the same content value of the same feed entry when returned by *feedparser* can differ. #### Impact It is not possible to use hash of the content to check if the content is changed over time. #### STR Run the test: ```py import unittest import textwrap import subprocess code = textwrap.dedent('''\ import feedparser parsed = feedparser.parse('https://savannah.gnu.org/news/atom.php?group=tar') entry = next(filter((lambda entry: entry.id == 'http://savannah.gnu.org/forum/forum.php?forum_id=8545'), parsed.entries)) content_value = entry.content[0].value print(content_value) ''') class MyTestCase(unittest.TestCase): def test(self): res1 = subprocess.check_output(['python', '-c', code]) res2 = subprocess.check_output(['python', '-c', code]) self.assertEqual(res1, res2) ``` #### Actual result ``` AssertionError: b'

[2158 chars]nput readonly="readonly" class="verbatim" size[2149 chars]\r\n' != b'

[2158 chars]nput value=" OLDNAME NEWNAME[:NEWID] " cla[2149 chars]\r\n' ``` #### Expected result The test is passed. #### Possible solution Preserve or determine an order of HTML attributes in a *content* of feed *entry*.

I am relatively new to sphinx and breathe in particular and trying to get my head around globbers.

Is it possible to "glob" for all files inside a directory and create a `doxygenfile` section for it? I would like to avoid writing something like
```rst
.. doxygenfile:: A.myfileEnding
   :project: myProject
   :path: src/param/A.myfileEnding
   :no-link:

.. doxygenfile:: B.myfileEnding
   :project: myProject

   :no-link:

   :no-link:

and would rather prefer a globber to include all files in `src/param/*` or all files named `*.myfileEnding` alternatively.

.. doxygenfile:: *.myfileEnding

I am one of the maintainers of Apache Airflow, licensed under the Apache License 2.0, and a contributor made us aware that we are dependent on “unidecode” a GPLv3 licensed python library, due to an indirect dependency on python-slugify (by means of python-nvd3).

issue_title: Feature request: allow interval jobs to be offset by a random value
issue_body:
When jobs are configured to execute at certain times, they tend to coalesce at one particular time. For example, executing 10 jobs at the top of the hour, another 10 once in at noon and once at midnight, and a third job that executes every minute will cause all of these jobs to run at the same time at noon and midnight, potentially causing a bottleneck in the number of threads.

This could be solved by introducing a new parameter to the interval trigger that would define a value in seconds by which each job would be offset.

Assuming these new imports:
``` python
from operator import add, sub
from random import randrange, choice
```
The constructor could have a new parameter value (`random_offset`) that would be at most equivalent to the configured interval:
``` python
self.random_offset = min(self.interval_length, random_offset)
```
Then, the `get_next_fire_time()` function could check if an offset is defined and modify the next execution time accordingly:
``` python
if self.random_offset:
    offset = timedelta(seconds=randrange(0, self.random_offset))
    op = choice((add, sub))
    next_fire_time = max(datetime.now(self.timezone), op(next_fire_time, offset))
```
I understand all this can be worked around by setting custom start times for each group of jobs so that they don't intersect but having an easy-to-configure parameter would be cool too. The UNIX Anacron scheduler uses a similar mechanic with the [`RANDOM_DELAY` option](http://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man5/anacrontab.5.html).

I did notice during testing of the above code that `get_next_fire_time()` is called both from `_get_run_times()` (in `job.py`) and `_process_jobs` in (in `base.py`), producing different times for each. I'm not sure if it's significant that these functions both receive the same next fire time.

Anyway, is this something you'd consider merging if I were to submit a PR? Thank you!

issue_title: scale/federate the rendezvous server?
issue_body:
Looking towards the future: magic-wormhole uses a single central rendezvous server to allow clients to find each other based upon just a small integer. If/when it becomes more popular, the traffic on this server might be an issue. How could we use multiple server hosts to avoid this bottleneck?

(note that this is independent of the "transit relay" server, which is morally equivalent to a TURN server and helps file transfers to work when both clients are behind NAT boxes, by gluing two TCP streams together)

There are two components to the rendezvous server. The first is channel allocation: the most common workflow is for `wormhole send` to allocate a channel by asking the server to assign one, and this needs to be unique (so the server needs to know a complete list of all channel IDs currently in use). These are called "nameplates" in the protocol and the code.

The second is "mailbox" management: a semi-persistent set of messages, and a publish-subscribe protocol for both sides to add and retrieve those messages. Each nameplate maps to a mailbox, each mailbox has a queue/set, and each client connects to a mailbox.

I think the channel-allocation part requires some sort of singular central table. Even if we get multiple servers involved, I think they'll need to talk to a single memcached or something.

The mailboxes could be sharded out among multiple machines, as long as there's a way for every client to get connected to the right machine. For example, we could use DNS round-robin or something to point to a fleet of servers, all of which can use that memcached instance to find out where a given mailbox lives. If a client connects to the wrong server, that server looks up the mailbox in the central table, learns about the correct server, then redirects the client to them. I think that means one non-sharded message per lookup, but everything else scales with the number of servers we use.

issue_body:
I have a list of 1500 odd addresses that I want  to geocode. My code for this is below:

```
import pandas as pd
import geocoder

data = pd.read_csv(r'path/to/dataset.csv', encoding='ISO-8859-1')
output_file = r'path/to/output_file.csv'

for i in range(len(data)):
    place = data['ADDRESS'][i]
    
    df_new = pd.DataFrame(index=[1], columns=['ADDRESS', 'add_no', 'modified_address', 'Lat', 'Lon', 'Result']) # create a new 1 line dataframe to store the geocoding results
    df_new['ADDRESS'][1] = place
    
    while True: 
        g = geocoder.google(place)
        
        if(len(g.geojson['features'])==0): # if the address is not recognized...
            place = place.split(' ',1)[1] # remove the first word from the address, and try again
        else:
            break
 
    df_new['modified_address'][1] = place
    df_new['Lat'][1] = g.latlng[0]

Firstly - thanks for creating `pyswagger`!

I'm having issues using URL special characters (e.g. `?`, `/` etc.) in path parameters with `pyswagger`.

Do you agree `pyswagger` should be URL quoting these parameters with e.g. [this function](https://docs.python.org/3/library/urllib.parse.html#urllib.parse.quote) before making requests?

As an example, if I try out the swagger UI [here](http://petstore.swagger.io/) and try the `GET /user/{username}` operation with username `asd?asd`, the request made is: `GET http://petstore.swagger.io/v2/user/asd%3Fasd`.

Now try the same thing with `pyswagger`, e.g.:

```
from pyswagger import App, Security
from pyswagger.contrib.client.requests import Client
app = App.create("http://petstore.swagger.io/v2/swagger.json")
client = Client(Security(app))
client.request(app.s('/user/{username}').get(username='asd?asd'))
```

and `pyswagger` does exactly `GET /v2/user/asd?asd` which is really a `GET` for username `asd` with a query parameter.

Looking at the code, I think `pyswagger` should be doing some quoting of path parameters around [here](https://github.com/mission-liao/pyswagger/blob/f706ba6e69c894098c619e549de2ed99d80261ff/pyswagger/io.py#L189).


`pyswagger` does handle query parameters correctly (I think `requests` is actually encoding them because they are passed to it separately, but `requests` can't encode the path as it's provided as one string so relies on `pyswagger` encoding the right parts):

```
...
client.request(app.s('/user/login').get(username='asd?asd', password='asd/asd'))

```

Results in `GET /v2/user/login?username=asd%3Fasd&password=asd%2Fasd` the same as if you use the [swagger UI](http://petstore.swagger.io/) for `/user/login` with the same parameters.

issue_title: Something assert by panflute about pandoc table can be False while pandoc is ok about it
issue_body:
I don't have much, and it might actually be a bug in pandoc, see [bug: docx (containing table) to native and docx to markdown then to native is hugely different - Google Groups](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/pandoc-discuss/nDFRvd8tj8k).

So far what I've got in some kind of MWE is this:

```json
[Table [] [AlignDefault,AlignDefault,AlignDefault] [0.0,0.0,0.0]
 [[Para [Str "x",Space,Str "y"]]
 ,[Para [Strong [Emph [Str "a",Space,Str "b"]]]]
 ,[Para [Strong [Emph [Str "Math"]]]]]
 []]
```

If you try `pandoc -t markdown -f native` on this file, pandoc would accept it. But when passing through panflute, it will complains `IndexError: list index out of range`.

But it could well be a bug from pandoc though.

issue_title: JSONField form: don't dump if value is already a string.
issue_body:
I have a Django model with a JSONField, and I want to create an instance of it though the admin site. 
In the JSONField I write the following:
```
[
    {"name": "foo"},
    {"name": "bar"}, 
] 
```
As expected, it's an invalid JSON (because of the trailing comma). 

What is unexpected is that when the form is re-rendered the content of the json field become:
`
" \r\n    [{\"name\": \"foo\", \r\n    {\"name\": \"bar\"}, \r\n]"
`
which is not json valid at all. 

This happens because the `prepare_value` function of the JSONField form is dumping the content of the field, when it's already a string. 

Django Version: *Django 1.10.6*

Database and version used: *MySQL 5.7*

Version: *Django-MySQL 1.1.1*

issue_title: Recommended approach to reset MAX7219 devices?
issue_body:
I'm in the process of trying to figure out why my large (>100 matrices) MAX7219 display board is encountering glitches. I've spent a lot of time experimenting with clock speeds, bus transfer rates, and I've got something very close to a stable state. Despite this, the most common glitch is that single matrices seem to shut off and on, or change contrast for some reason, and it's not clear why. 

I want to do some debugging but aside from rerunning `get_device()`, which is the only high level abstraction that I can find which runs the `device`'s `show()` method (it's a `max7219` class instance) I don't have any means of resetting the devices. There's also the `clear()` method... I'm really not sure which one of the two, or both, are responsible for resetting the device to a stable state again.

My hypothesis is that if I'm rendering frames on the display at a constant rate, I could inject the command that successfully cleans up the glitch every second (framerate+1 frames) and it would prevent the glitched matrices from appearing glitched to the human eye, with minimal impact on performance.

I'm also analyzing the signals using a protocol analyzer and oscilloscope to see if there's some other reason why the MAX7219 modules are doing this strange behavior. From this perspective, the hypothesis is that the devices being affected by the glitch are missing a clock cycle or something similar. Not sure about this yet. Power reads clean on the oscilloscope, signals are 5V0 using a boost converter from the pi's 3V3 outputs, everything is wired in parallel except the DOUT to DIN pins on each module. I'm using PCBs with 4x MAX7219 chips each, the typical ones found on Amazon and Ebay. There's no significant ringing on any of the signal pulses as far as I can see. At this point there's nothing I can really change with respect to wiring, the clock speed, or the SPI MTU chunk size that makes it glitch any less, so I'm hoping I might be able to get closer to a solution by resetting the devices as quickly as possible on a regular interval.

issue_title: API Documentation missing
Update codemirror to current version
License and copyright
issue_body:
Hi,
in the code there is no information on the copyright years applied to the project, and also the license text is required, since BSD is a too generic name, and the BSD licenses have changed during the years (so you should select one and include the verbatim text somewhere in the source files). These problems prevent the inclusion of this package in Debian

issue_title: Unable to import psycopg2 error in brew install.
issue_body:
I installed eralchemy using brew, but when I attempt to run it, I'm getting the following error:

```
$ eralchemy -i 'postgresql+psycopg2://dakota@localhost:5432/logbook' -o logbook.pdf
Please install psycopg2 using "pip install psycopg2".
```

So for the sake of not reporting an obvious issue; I went ahead and ran the command as requested:

```
$ pip install psycopg2
Requirement already satisfied (use --upgrade to upgrade): psycopg2 in /usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages
```

And of course, opening a Python terminal and importing psycopg2 works. I even tried creating a virtual environment, installing pscyopg2 there, and running eralchemy. No joy. 

ERAlchemy is working for me now; I uninstalled the brew installation and used pip to install the latest and am now good to go. Not sure if anyone else has had this issue; and I don't really know how to resolve it.

issue_title: O365 - ErrorExceededConnectionCount
issue_body:
Hi, 
I've been using this exchangelib library for the past 3 months to monitor a mailbox but over the past few days the script stopped running because it was running into an issue with concurrent connections.  The exchange environment is in O365, anyone aware of if there is a way to explicitly terminate the connection?  The mailbox was moved which should have reset the connection.   

exchangelib.errors.ErrorExceededConnectionCount: You have exceeded the available concurrent connections for your account.  Try again once your other requests have completed.

This issue only occurs when using impersonation, i'm not sure if this is an exchange issue or a library issue.  The code has been running consistently for months.

`account = Account(primary_smtp_address=smtp_address,
                       credentials=Credentials(username=username, password=password),
                       autodiscover=True,
                       access_type=access_type)`

issue_title: Can fix many of the backwards incompatibilities introduced in 1.10.0
issue_body:
The breaking changes were introduced to fix errors in app loading that were caused by Django 1.9's app loading changes. Specifically, importing models in an app's init.py started raising an exception. Thus, the reversion top-level api function in init.py were moved out.

Turns out that didn't actually fix all the issues, since the register() call was still triggering the bug when imported in an AppConfig. The real solution was to lazy-import all the model references.

Everything but the VersionAdmin import backwards-incompatibility can be fixed, and we'll avoid any more breaking changes in doing so.

issue_title: #text shows up when value is false-ish
issue_body:
First off, this is a great package.  Thanks for writing it.

Second, when I use "#text" and "@attrs", and I point the text at something that evaluates to False, the XML rendered looks different!

    >>> ebaysdk.utils.dict2xml({'Item': {'WeightMajor': {'#text': 0, '@attrs': {'unit': 'oz'}}}})
    '<Item><WeightMajor unit="oz"><#text>0</#text></WeightMajor></Item>'

    >>> ebaysdk.utils.dict2xml({'Item': {'WeightMajor': {'#text': 1, '@attrs': {'unit': 'oz'}}}})
    '<Item><WeightMajor unit="oz">1</WeightMajor></Item>'

I suspect it is because of this section in dict2xml:

            if isinstance(root[key], dict):
                attrs, value = attribute_check(root[key])

                if not value:
                    value = dict2xml(root[key])

That `if not value` should probably something more specific.

issue_title: Add support for Home Assistant Entitiy Registry
issue_body:
Home Assistant has an Entity Registry where you can "statically" assign entity ids to device serial numbers or other unique ids.

Since the name resolving through pyhomematic takes quite long when starting Home Assistant it would be cool if we could register a device in the entity registry once it's name has been fetched, so resolving that device's name can be skipped in the future. The Philips Hue platform already contains an example on how to use the Entity Registry.

Benefits people would get from this:

* Faster startup times (entities will be available instantly at startup)
* Stable entity ids (IMHO this is a huge thing)
* Renaming entity ids for usage with Home Assistant without renaming them on CCU
* Less load on CCU during HASS restarts

I think since all HM devices have a serial number this would be a good unique id to use in the Entity Registry (maybe together with the channel number, if required).

For more information see the developer docs:

https://developers.home-assistant.io/docs/en/entity_registry_index.html

issue_title: Add suport for other styles?
issue_body:
Hi!

I discovered that there were [many different ways](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#CSI_codes) to [stylize the terminal police](https://misc.flogisoft.com/bash/tip_colors_and_formatting).

Among the most used, and most implemented in libraries similar to `ansimarkup`:
* Underline
* Italic
* Strikethrough
* Blink
* Reverse
* Hidden

Downsides:
* Some terminal may not implement it correctly
* We may need to manage the combinations of several styles at the same time

Do you think it would make sense to add them to the libraries?

issue_title: Cox and KM give different survivals in 0.10.0
issue_body:
Hi,

sorry for reposting in a new issue, but I am not sure if #289 (now closed) is still being watched :)

Here are my steps:

1) prepare the data, scaling the continuous variables with sklearn's minmaxscaler. I also tried to remove my own scaling, but the results don't really change
2) pass the data through patsy and prepare the input to lifelines
3) fit a cox model
4) obtain the mean matrix (myPatsyMatrix.mean()) and use it to predict the Cox lifetime
4) perform a KM mesurement on the same population
6) compare the cox prediction and the KM measurement

With the previous version of lifelines, things were ok as long as I specified "normalize=False". Now the cox prediction and the KM measurement are significantly off.

I had a look at the changes in 0.10.0, and I am not sure I understand what is being done.

What I see in the code is:

1) you apply some transformation to the inputs (using `utils.normalize(fitData, fitDataMean, fitDataStd)`) and fit the model. this makes my own use of the minmaxscaler ineffective, but I don't see how it could create problems.
2) at prediction time, the user gives you a set of values for the features of the model. What should happen now is that you use the same transformation you used before fitting, e.g. `utils.normalize(newData, fitDataMean, fitDataStd)`, then feed the result to the calculation.

If I follow the execution flow starting e.g. from CoxPHFitter::predict_survival_function, I can't really see where you apply the transformation to the inputs X. Or am I misunderstanding the code?

Assuming I am missing something in the code and all transformations are done correctly, this means that, when predicting on a point that is exactly the mean of the fitted dataset, all partial hazards do not contribute anymore, and the only relevant term is the baseline. Doesn't this mean that the baseline survival from cox  should be fairly similar to KM's measurement?

Andrea.

issue_title: BetaGeo Fitter conditional_probability_alive() variants for case when frequency = 0
issue_body:
I'm running into a situation where the results of my BGF probability score defaults to 1 for users that have no frequency (one-time purchasers). Like most companies, I have customers who have been "alive" for years, but haven't made a second purchase. In their case, I'd rather assume that their probability is near-zero than follow the model's assumption.

The [Bruce Hardie paper](http://www.brucehardie.com/notes/021/palive_for_BGNBD.pdf) that's referenced in the code for `beta_geo_fitter.py` addresses this assumption and suggests two potential variants:

> We note that P(alive) = 1 for a customer who made no purchases in the interval (0, T]; this follows from the model’s assumptions that death occurs after a purchase and that customers are alive at the beginning of the observation period. Those interested in the P(alive) metric may view this as a shortcoming of the model.

Have you considered adding these variants to account for customers with no frequency? I feel like both of the ones he offers up would involve fairly minor additions to the function (a couple optional arguments and slight tweaks to the formula), but wanted to get your thoughts on whether I'm thinking about this wrong, or if there are other approaches I should consider before I submit a PR.

issue_title: Failed to load resource: net::ERR_TIMED_OUT
issue_body:
Hello Flask gurus!
This is not an actual issue with flask-restplus but I'm a little bit desperate about this issue. I've posted it on Stackoverflow and I'm resharing it here as I hope to get some help / advices on how to troubleshoot this.

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/46174299/failed-to-load-resource-neterr-timed-out

We've built an API using Flask Restplus and it's usually running fine. It's using Flask internal webserver (Werkzeug). We have a web application built with Angular.js which is making http requests to our API. In some occasions, the web application fails to load its page and the following error message appears in Chrome console:

> Failed to load resource: net::ERR_TIMED_OUT

To be completely honest, I am not sure if this is related to:

 - The web application itself making an invalid call to the API
 - The API itself which is not responding in a timely manner (it still appears up and running when we get that error message)

I don't know how to troubleshoot this and I'm looking for some guidance to narrow downs the investigation to the faulty component. Any suggestion?

***Update:*** I've been able to reproduce the error by chance. Chrome Network tab did not show any information about the resource which timed out. It only showed info about resources loaded successfully. 

However the Console tab did show several endpoints which timed out. Example:

[![Error Message][1]][1]

Because there was several endpoints failing, my guess is the problem is on the back-end (not the web app making the http requests), and the API did not respond fast enough. What I don't know is which back-end component is responsible for the issue (the database, the server, the network, another API that we are calling in the backend, etc...)

  [1]: https://i.stack.imgur.com/7zont.png

issue_title: Is aprslib Intended to Generate Valid APRS packets?
issue_body:
Per the [APRS Protocol](http://www.aprs.org/doc/APRS101.PDF):

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/331540/23585999/41e3ae10-0141-11e7-801d-98e3b9b7a516.png)

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/331540/23586000/4e0a057c-0141-11e7-9c90-a7c13a8e249e.png)

However with the following latitude and longitude the resulting string when using `aprslib.packets.PositionReport()` is:

```
3359.7036
11826.9392
2017-03-05 01:13:28,890 - APRS - INFO - KB1LQC-0>KB1LQC-1:!335942.22N/1182656.35En/A=000023Faraday Wireless Node
```

However, NMEA GGA data is sent down as ddMM.mmmm and dddMM.mmmm:
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/331540/23586039/f3046694-0141-11e7-8c89-f53a332cb805.png)

This means that we shouldn't have to do any conversions, just rounding and some zero stuffing to get

latitude = ddMM.mm
Longitude = dddMM.mm

A correct string sent to APRS-IS with non aprslib code with similar coordinates is:
`KB1LQC-2>GPSFDY,QAR,KB1LQC-1,qAS,KB1LQC:!3359.70N/11826.94Wn.../000/A=000037Faraday Wireless Node`

This shows up exactly where I'd expect it to. I cannot get aprslib to generate APRS compliant strings. Is this the intended function?

issue_title: Translation strings for different sentence syntax
issue_body:
Hi,

I was translating your app to Turkish but there are some problems with the translation mechanism due to differences in preposition syntax.

For example:
There exists a string:

RSS Feed of pingbacks on

Possibly you should have used {% trans "RSS Feed of pingbacks on" %} {{variable}} ...

But Turkish sentences with such preposition (on) uses syntax:
{{variable}} on RSS Feed pingbacks

So I can't translate this sentence. If you could use
{% blocktrans %}RSS Feed of pingbacks on {{variable}}{%endblocktrans%}

Then I could translate it into:
{{variable}} on RSS Feed pingbacks
{{variable}} üzerinde RSS Besleme geri bağlantıları

The problematic words that are prepositions: of, to, in, from, with, without, about, against, among, at, before, after, etc...

http://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Turkish/Cases

I know that it'd be problematic to fix them all but I may give it a try and you could keep this problem in mind for further translation tags.

issue_title: mixed prefetch_related() and select_related()
issue_body:
Hi @eamigo86, thanks for sharing this!

I was struggling with Django ORM errors like:
> Invalid field name(s) given in select_related: 'my_field'. Choices are: my_other_field, another_one, ...

 In particular, this happens when both querying AND filtering by a many-to-many or one-to-many relation field.

If i'm not missing something, there seems to be a subtle bug over `utils.recursive_params()` that leads to such fields being used as arguments for `prefetch_related` (expected) and simultaneously for `select_related` (unexpected) if they're "visited" more than once within the recursion.

I made a pull request #42 which fixes this issue for me, by adjusting [this](https://github.com/eamigo86/graphene-django-\extras/blob/master/graphene_django_extras/utils.py#L277-L281) duplicate-preventing conditional that misidentifies already-added fields meant for `prefetch_related` as fields meant for `select_related`.

issue_title: [Superfences] Pass fenced code block config (hl_lines, linenums, ...) to custom formatter func

Hi @facelessuser, I absolutely love your MD extensions. They're powering large parts of realpython.com and your documentation has been a joy to work with 😃 

I'm using the custom formatter functions in `SuperFences` to customize rendering and syntax highlighting. I'm looking to find a way to access fenced code block config like `hl_lines` and `linenums` from the formatter function.

As best as I can tell, a small change in the custom formatter function API to start passing through those config values would achieve this quite easily:

https://github.com/facelessuser/pymdown-extensions/blob/881e852ecfd06449fe608932c3abd4deffe2005e/pymdownx/superfences.py#L370

```python
    def process_nested_block(self, ws, content, start, end):
        """Process the contents of the nested block."""

        self.last = ws + self.normalize_ws(content)
        code = None
        for entry in reversed(self.extension.superfences):
            if entry["test"](self.lang):
                #

                code = entry["formatter"](self.rebuild_block(self.code), self.lang)

```

issue_title: Using of default instead of initial to pre-populate html fields
issue_body:
Hi, 

It seems that swagger is using the "default" attribute instead of the "initial" value to prepopulate the html fields.

If I have a field like this `quantity = IntegerField(default=1)`, it gets rendered as:
```<input class="parameter" minlength="0" name="quantity" placeholder="" value="1" type="text">```. the field becomes "non requrired".

And if my field is `quantity = IntegerField(initial=1)`, it gets rendered as:
```<input class="parameter required" minlength="1" name="quantite" placeholder="(required)" value="" type="text">```. The initial value is not taken into account.

According to [django rest docs](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/fields/#core-arguments), "initial" attribute is:
> A value that should be used for pre-populating the value of HTML form fields.

while "default" is:
> If set, this gives the default value that will be used for the field if no input value is supplied. [...] 
Note that setting a default value implies that the field is not required. Including both the default and required keyword arguments is invalid and will raise an error.

I would like my field to be required and have and "html" default value of 1.

Is this a bug of expected behavior for swagger? If it's a bug, please just point me in the right direction and I'll correct it and make a pull request.

Thanks

issue_title: Chalice generates package
issue_body:
I have a non-chalice lambda function that imports numpy and scipy. I am able to zip up my custom lambda function package to be under 30 MB, and thus I can deploy this to AWS lambda without exceeding the 50 MB limit. However, when I try to use chalice and include both numpy (1.13.3) and scipy (0.19.1) in the requirements.txt, it tells me that the package size is 62.2 MB and that I cannot deploy the package because it is too large.

I might be able to hack around this using the vendor directory and copying over a small subset of manylinux1_x86_64-compiled scipy files that I actually need, but the simple solution using requirements.txt would be much easier to maintain and execute.

Anyone have any thoughts on chalice's 62.2 MB vs. my <30 MB package size?

issue_title: Python boxsdk[jwt] Post request hangs when body is a StringIO
issue_body:
The issue comes from the requests library as described here:
https://github.com/requests/requests/issues/2872
boxsdk[jwt] is still using an older version so the fix is not incorporated.
There are two possible solutions:
Upgrade to version of the requests library (could break other things though) or ammend the documentation that the file object has to be a true file object and not a StringIO or ByteIO object.

issue_title: restoring `elementwise_grad`
issue_body:
@matthewdhoffman and others have convinced me that we should restore the `elementwise_grad` function (possibly with a better name).

Basically, I think `grad` and `elementwise_grad` should both exist and have almost the same implementation; the only difference would be that `grad` would raise an error for non-scalar-valued functions. (The docstrings would also be different, and `elementwise_grad` could explain that it returns the diagonal elements of the Jacobian under the promise that the Jacobian is diagonal, etc.)

I think having that behavior in `grad` is confusing and might hide bugs in users' code that they would otherwise immediately notice.

Thoughts?

issue_title: No retries for TooManyRequestsException when using organizations client
issue_body:
It looks like the botocore isn't using its retry logic when it receives a TooManyRequestsException from the organizations API.  I confirmed this issue with our AWS TAM, but wanted to report it here as well.

I am getting the exception when using this psuedo-code as a part of a Lambda function:

```
backoff_config = Config(
    retries=dict(
        max_attempts=15
    )
)

# create an org client
org_client = org_owner_session.client('organizations',
                                      config=backoff_config)
    
# get an account list from the org owner
all_accounts = org_client.list_accounts()['Accounts']
```

When the above code is executed by several Lambda containers simultaneously, the following error is produced:

```
An error occurred (TooManyRequestsException) when calling the ListAccounts operation: AWS Organizations can't complete your request because another request is already in progress. Try again later.
````

It does not appear that boto3 is retrying the request after this exception is caught.

I can workaround the issue by implementing my own backoff logic, but I would prefer to use the retry functionality of boto3 instead.

Let me know if I can provide more information.  Thanks.

issue_title: Transparent decompression of downloaded objects with gzip Content-Encoding
issue_body:
**Problem**: Most modern web browsers, curl (`curl --compressed "http://..."`), wget (`wget -S --header="accept-encoding: gzip" wordpress.com "http://..."`), and other tools will accept gzip-encoded web data and transparently decompress it before it is used or written out. boto3 has no built-in functionality to do this.

**Proposed solution**: It would be helpful if boto3's `download_file()` could transparently decompress any object stored with `Content-Encoding: gzip` metadata. This would obviate the need for downstream tools to navigate decompression with streams, etc.

If not the default, perhaps an option could be passed to`ExtraArgs` to enable transparent gzip decompression. Having an option would be similar to a capable browser informing a server to send gzip via a header of `Accept-Encoding: gzip`.  Since S3 cannot set the encoding based on the `Accept-Encoding` header sent, it would be helpful if boto3 could better handle common `Content-Encoding` types. The `gzip` type seems like a reasonable place to start, but perhaps `download_file()` could accept an encoding handler as a kwarg to address various other content types. 

Along with transparent decompression during download, it would be helpful to also specify an encoding handler for `upload_file()` that would act prior to upload and set the `ExtraArg` `ContentEncoding` accordingly.

issue_title: deisctl should group mesos components
issue_body:
Currently each of the four mesos components are managed as standalone entries in `deisctl`, which is more verbose than needed. 
```
$ deisctl stop mesos
● ▴ ■
■ ● ▴ Stopping Mesos...
▴ ■ ●
Marathon Framwork...
deis-mesos-marathon.service: inactive/dead                           
Mesos Slave...
deis-mesos-slave.service: inactive/dead                              
Mesos Master...
deis-mesos-master.service: inactive/dead                             
Zookeeper...
deis-zookeeper.service: inactive/dead                                
Done.
```

```console
$ deisctl stop mesos
● ▴ ■
■ ● ▴ Stopping Mesos...

Mesos with Marathon...
deis-mesos-marathon.service: inactive/dead

issue_title: bug(test): during the test if the user app is stopped it continues running
issue_body:
instead of fail.

Steps to reproduce:
- during the test enter in any node.
- stop the app.
- the suite will continue to run instead of throwing an error


Why is relevant?
In case of an error in the deployed app it will restarted until the next output:
```
Mar 22 04:31:15 deis-01 systemd[1]: configsample_v9.cmd.1.service holdoff time over, scheduling restart.
```

then the service will stop and the suite will continue running
