issue_title: Upgrading to 0.7.0 broke blog thumbnails options
issue_body:
I had added a Thumbnail Option in the Django Admin interface in 0.6.3,( for Cmsplugin_Filer_Image) and on my blog posts, i had my image set with it as the thumbnail option. When I upgraded to 0.7.0, my thumbnails disappeared, when I looked at the post editor, the drop down selectors for the thumbnail options are now empty. (MAIN IMAGE THUMBNAIL, MAIN IMAGE FULL). Before the upgrade, they showed a list of Thumbnail Option models.

I tried adding a new thumbnail option, but it doesn't show up in the dropdown either

The thumbnail images don't render. If I edit the post and then save it again, the thumbnail then shows, it uses the default 120x120 (or whatever its set to the in settings), since there is no other option in the dropdown.

issue_title: invoking the server command with --boilerplate option doesn't configures boilerplates
issue_body:
Example of a failure https://travis-ci.org/aldryn/aldryn-newsblog/jobs/77465895#L733
the configuration of that particular test https://github.com/aldryn/aldryn-boilerplates/pull/14/files#diff-2b5bde3dd5ae5bc227010549100ba6a8R27

The issue is related to the fact that django runserver command spwans a new process/thread which gets non unicoded arguments in sys.argv.
Also if some of the arguments was passed twice (or was duplicated because of that spawn) docopt wasn't able to map arguments correctly.

issue_title: setup.py presumptuous (and possibly wrong) about how it will be used
issue_body:
There are a number of assumptions made in https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/master/setup.py that may not necessarily be true, or at least may force users to use it in a very specific way.  Here are a few examples:
- [ ] There is a reference to `pip` at https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/master/setup.py#L35, but the user may not be using `pip`, nor should `pip` be a requirement.
- [ ] There are references to `egg-info`.  What about wheels?  What if I'm not using eggs?

cc @scottpurdy

issue_title: Q: should SP, TP use vector rather than array?
issue_body: it seems it would be more convenient, otherwise a nit.

issue_title: Segments with zero synapses should be deleted
issue_body:
The [current implementation of temporal memory](https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/16e9a911e70e161586e2d04762b1b92d255ae468/src/nupic/algorithms/TemporalMemory.cpp) has a mechanism for deleting synapses that reach zero permanence, but not for deleting segments that reach zero synapses. Deleting such segments would improve space and speed performance.

@subutai @scottpurdy

issue_title: Release script does not update the code source in GitHub
issue_body:
I noticed an issue when running the release.sh script. The tag/release is created in GitHub but the code source file containing the updated version number, `__init__.py` is not updated according to GitHub. It _does_ get updated in my local working directory when `release.sh` is run but for some reason the change to `__init__.py` does not get pushed to GitHub.

I thought that it was possibly `git push origin $TAG_NAME` that is used and required to push the newly created tag (and thus a new "version" in GitHub, required for the Docker hook) but removing that command in the script to simply be `git push origin` does not push the programmatically created tag.

Any advice on resolving this issue, @ibnesayeed?

issue_title: Reduce coupling between watchmaker params and the salt formulas
issue_body:
Right now there are several watchmaker params that write salt grains that are specific to paths used within the salt formulas. This means the watchmaker code needs to know internal details of how the salt formulas work. This proposal would be to separate that coupling by:

1. Modify the salt formulas to read a configuration item (pillar) that specifies the grain that contains the configuration setting, and to get the setting from that grain.
2. Modify watchmaker to write grains in the salt worker to the `watchmaker` grain namespace. In the [current code](https://github.com/plus3it/watchmaker/blob/develop/src/watchmaker/workers/salt.py#L162), this means anywhere we call `_set_grain()`, the `grain` would have a value of `watchmaker`.

issue_title: make docs for transforms.functional auto generate
Add 'level' to DatasetID
issue_body:
#### Problem description

We currently have no way of describing data at certain pressure levels. In the NUCAPS reader I do something semi-hacky where I append the pressure level (in millibars) to the dataset name. This results in ~100 new dataset entries for each dataset that can only be identified by knowing the exact name. Due to how NUCAPS does things these datasets are 1D arrays iirc. It is also possible to request the generic dataset and get a 2D array that is `(pressure level, rows * cols)`.

#### Proposed Solution

Add a `level` field to `DatasetID`. Default value can be `None`, otherwise it is a number of the millibars pressure level of the dataset. Biggest issue with this is we should really only support one type of "level": Do we do millibar pressure level or do we do altitude in meters?

issue_title: Add Maximum Inner Product Search
issue_body:
See http://arxiv.org/pdf/1410.5410v2.pdf

This is pretty easy to implement. I will probably do it myself, but in case I don't get to it, I wanted to jot some notes down and this seems like a good place to do it.

The basic algorithm is very simple and can be used in Annoy Angular mode with the following modifications, assuming we use the recommended m=2, U=0.75 parameters in the paper:

Initialization:
 - Add two to the input dimension before any add_item has been called, and pad the last two dimensions with 0s in add_item.

When it is time to `build()`:
 - First, find the norm of the largest vector in the input set and compute a scale parameter U / ||x_max||, and then re-scale all vectors by this factor. Re-scaling the universe does not change the argmax of any maximum inner product.
 - While rescaling, set the extra two dimensions of each vector to [0.5 - ||x_i||^2, 0.5 - ||x_i||^4]
 - Resume the standard build process.

When it is time to query:
 - Just append two zeros to the query vector. That's it. The result will be the maximum dot product, instead of the maximum cosine distance, because of the brilliant / insane proofs in the paper above.

issue_title: Redshift: Serializable isolation violation
issue_body:
We often get an error when multiple jobs are attempting to update the table_updates table in Redshift at the same time. The error is thrown when the PostgresTarget output class calls self.exists just after writing to the table_updates table, when another task is writing to the table. The DB cannot guarantee the results of the query, and this gets thrown:

```
Runtime error:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/luigi/worker.py", line 181, in run
    new_deps = self._run_get_new_deps()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/luigi/worker.py", line 119, in _run_get_new_deps
    task_gen = self.task.run()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/luigi/contrib/redshift.py", line 256, in run
    output.touch(connection)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/luigi/postgres.py", line 166, in touch
    assert self.exists(connection)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/luigi/postgres.py", line 177, in exists
    (self.update_id,)
InternalError: 1023
DETAIL:  Serializable isolation violation on table - 276701, transactions forming the cycle are: 8680257, 8680319 (pid:28411)
```

I'm considering making a pull request to add to the error handling in luigi/postgres.py, to catch the InternalError, wait for a time period, and then retry the select.

Firstly, does anyone know how best to catch the error? I don't know where in  psycopy2.errorcodes the error code is, and don't want to just catch any InternalError.

Secondly, does such a backoff approach to handling this have any merit?

issue_title: Tasks that complete in <1s may display as RUNNING or PENDING in task history
issue_body:
Using MySQL backend, I am seeing tasks that have completed in either "RUNNING" or "PENDING" state in the task_history webpage.  Looking at the db there is a row marking these tasks as "DONE" but their timestamps clash with the displayed event.  Clearly the timestamps aren't sub-second precision.

E.g.

    mysql> select id, event_name, ts from task_events where task_id=61;
    +-----+------------+---------------------+
    | id  | event_name | ts                  |
    +-----+------------+---------------------+
    | 181 | PENDING    | 2015-04-22 15:10:02 |
    | 230 | RUNNING    | 2015-04-22 15:12:38 |
    | 231 | DONE       | 2015-04-22 15:12:38 |
    +-----+------------+---------------------+
    3 rows in set (0.00 sec)

Modern MySQL should support this.  I have found this stackoverflow question suggesting the issue is with SQLAlchemy: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/29711102/sqlalchemy-mysql-millisecond-or-microsecond-precision

Other than fixing this in SQLAlchemy it might work to simply sort task_event rows by timestamp *and* primary key, assuming the events are inserted in true order.

issue_title: Unexpected behavior with command-line overrides of parameters with default values
issue_body:
Using the following code example as test.py:
```
import luigi

class MyTask(luigi.Task):
    param1 = luigi.IntParameter()
    param2 = luigi.BoolParameter(default=False)

    def requires(self):
        if self.param1 > 0:
            yield MyTask(self.param1 - 1)

    def complete(self):
        return False

    def run(self):
        pass

if __name__ == '__main__':
    luigi.run()
```

if I run `python test.py MyTask --param1 1` I'll see the output I expect, with the following run summary:

```
===== Luigi Execution Summary =====

Scheduled 2 tasks of which:
* 1 ran successfully:
    - 1 MyTask(param1=0, param2=False)
* 1 failed:
    - 1 MyTask(param1=1, param2=False)

This progress looks :( because there were failed tasks

===== Luigi Execution Summary =====
```

but if I add `--param2` to set param2 to true in my root task, I get

```
===== Luigi Execution Summary =====

Scheduled 2 tasks of which:
* 1 ran successfully:
    - 1 MyTask(param1=0, param2=True)
* 1 failed:
    - 1 MyTask(param1=1, param2=True)

This progress looks :( because there were failed tasks

===== Luigi Execution Summary =====
```

which shows that I've actually changed param2 in _all_ tasks of the same class. This is pretty far from expected and normal behavior for member parameters. I usually only expect to be able to make global changes via parameters set in Config classes. This behavior seems to be new with #1182.

issue_title: Demonstrate how to use the trained model with new data in examples
issue_body:
This is more of a feature request around examples. 

It'd be very useful to extend the examples to demonstrate how one might use the trained models on new data. This is already done for the generative models such as the NLP City Name Generator but when it comes to the classifiers the examples are currently only concerned with creating networks and training. They never show how the model can be used on new data.

For example, the `lstm.py` currently finishes with the line where `mode.fit` is called. What I'm suggesting is to extend the example code to include a case where the model is used on new data. 
````python
.....
# Training

model.fit(trainX, trainY, validation_set=(testX, testY), show_metric=True,
          batch_size=32)

# Use

issue_title: Epsilon Saturates in 1 Step Q runner
issue_body:
This seems to be a conceptual issue. In the pacman example the e-greedy policy is annealed over time. 
If the network is run for more than a few hours, epsilon eventually goes to 0 and the distribution of sampled values oscillates between only two values 0.5 and 0.1. The reward for any game with an epsilon of .5 is the same. I slowed down the annealing process but that's just a hack to extend the training time. I should continually exploit more as training continues, but how should I avoid a zero epsilon?

issue_title: Parameter in "path" and in "formData" do not work
issue_body:
### Description
When defining an endpoint with parameters in _path_ and in _formData_ and enabling _strict_validation_ function _validate_formdata_parameter_list_ validates only parameter in _path_. Thereby parameter in _formData_ is ignored.

### Expected behaviour
Function _validate_formdata_parameter_list_ should validate all parameters (form, files) but not parameters in _path_ .

### Actual behaviour
the validation fails.

### Steps to reproduce
Define endpoint like:
```
/items/{item_id}/data:
    post:
      description: "submit data"
      operationId: "microservice.api.items_data_post"
      consumes:
      - "multipart/form-data"
      produces:
      - "application/json"
      parameters:
      - name: "item_id"
        description: "item id"
        type: "integer"
        in: "path"
        required: true
      - name: "data"
        description: "some data"
        type: "file"
        in: "formData"
        required: true
      responses:
        200:
          description: "Success"
          schema:
            $ref: "#/definitions/successResponse"
        default:
          description: "Error"
          schema:
            $ref: "#/definitions/errorResponse"
definitions:
  errorResponse:
    required:
    - "message"
    properties:
      message:
        description: "error message"
        type: "string"
  successResponse:
    type: "object"
```
Python function / endpoint:
```
def items_data_post(item_id, data):
    return successResponse(), 200
```
### Additional info:
some additional output from `connexion.decorators.validation.py`, line 262:

------------------------------

------------------------------
path parameters

Code for output above:
```
if self.strict_validation:
                query_errors = self.validate_query_parameter_list(request)

                        print('connexion :: query_errors', query_errors)

In more concise language, obviously. :) Thoughts?

issue_title: get_function_arguments() behavior changes between python 2.7 and python 3.5
issue_body:
Function get_function_parameters from connexion/decorators/parameters.py changes its behavior from python 2.7 to python 3.5:

```python
def get_function_arguments(function):  # pragma: no cover
    """
    Returns the list of arguments of a function
    :type function: Callable
    :rtype: list[str]
    """
    if six.PY3:
        return list(inspect.signature(function).parameters)
    else:
        return inspect.getargspec(function).args
```

If the parameter function is a decorated function (i.e basicauth example) with parameters in python 2.7 the result is always an empty list while in python 3.5 the result is the list with the actual parameters of the decorated function.

issue_title: possible bug in z3 ddnf implementation
issue_body:
There is some difference between the ddnf algorithm described in [ddnf's paper](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/wp-content/uploads/2015/11/An-Efficient-Data-Structure-for-Header-Spaces.pdf) and the z3 implementation of it.
Due to [this line](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/cd24535e51263d8447efea9b917977863b97d8e5/src/muz/ddnf/ddnf.cpp#L195) in the the implementation, the data structure that is created after inserting a list of ternary bit vectors (tbvs) might be different from what is described in the paper. 
For example, after inserting `11`, `1x`, `x1`, the expected data structure according to the paper (or at least my understanding of it) should look like this:
```
      xx
    /    \
   x1    1x
    \    /
      11
```

However, here is the result of calling `display` function on `ddnf_core` after inserting the above tbvs (in the given order): 
```
node[0: xx 2 3]
node[1: 11]
node[2: x1 1]
node[3: 1x]
```
As you can see the implementation does not add `11` as child of `1x`.
If I insert the tbvs in the reverse order, (`x1`, `1x`, `11`), the result is as expected:
```
node[0: xx 1 2]
node[1: x1 3]
node[2: 1x 3]
node[3: 11]
```

It looks like a bug in the implementation to me. I was wondering whether that is indeed the case or there is something that I am missing?

issue_title: z3 solver's solution space of incremental solving
ML API: mk_int: check for overflow
issue_body:
I just discovered a nasty unsoundness https://github.com/verifast/verifast/issues/138 in my tool VeriFast, caused by the fact that `Z3native.mk_int n s`, on platforms where OCaml's `int` type is 63 bits but C's `int` type is 32 bits (which today probably means any platform except Cygwin32; it definitely includes macOS), silently truncates its argument `n`.

The docs for `Z3.mk_int` do (somewhat vaguely) warn about not all integers being supported, but still I would suggest putting a run-time check into `Z3native.mk_int`, something like:
```c
if ((int)Int_val(a1) != Int_val(a1)) { /* throw an OCaml exception */ }
```

Since the Z3native stubs are generated automatically, I guess this should be fixed in `update_api.py`. Furthermore, I guess this issue really applies to each API function that takes an OCaml `int`.

Alternatively, of course, versions of the Z3 C APIs could be implemented and used that take machine words instead of C `int`s.

    (set-option :rewriter.hi_div0 false)

issue_title: Encoding detection results in performance difference with other clients
issue_body:
## Long story short

When using aiohttp for fetching pages I found strange performance problems. We started comparing timings with other clients like curl and requests and difference was significant. For other clients fetching page was 6 times faster compared to aiohttp. After some digging we found that problem was using method "text" that was used encoding detection from chardet. 

Requests is also using chardet, but the difference is that it's skipping it if content-type contains word "text" by using 'ISO-8859-1'. https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/master/requests/utils.py#L362

## Expected behaviour

Matching behavior to other popular clients.

## Actual behaviour

Huge performance hit for pages without explicit encoding. For example "Content-Type: text/html"
For 300kB pages time difference for using encoding and not is 8s to 2s respectively. (using method "text" instead of "read"), and for 1.3MB page difference is 33s to 4.5s.

## Steps to reproduce

I'm sorry I cannot disclose the page that I used for testing. 

## Your environment
I tested it on two environments
Linux 4.8 Ubuntu 16.10 Python 3.6
aiohttp==1.0.5
chardet==2.3.0

Linux 4.8 Ubuntu 16.10 Python 3.5.2
aiohttp==2.0.6
chardet==3.0.1
^ for that environment problem was smaller by 30%

issue_title: 2.x: ClientSession read_timeout should not be cumulative
issue_body:
read_timeout is usually implemented as time from receiving each chunk of data.  This is to prevent servers which are not data back w/in a certain amount of time.

2.x changed the behavior for this to be cumulative, which means that short downloads will work ok, but large downloads will fail even for servers that are still responding correctly.  I have never seen read_timeout be a cumulative timeout.  This change is similar to https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/1907 in that it's a change in behavior that no-one will expect.  

Further there's no real reason to apply a cumulative read_timeout as this will be handled by the `_request` `timeout` parameter.

In a minimum this needs to be changed to have a flag to disable this behavior and make it work like before as to do the previous behavior clients will have to take over the read timeout handling.

issue_title: handle 420 status code
issue_body:
If a client sends too many requests Airbrake will first sample these requests, drop some errors and return a 420.

requests __does not__ currently support an [HTTP 420 response code](https://github.com/requests/requests/blob/master/requests/status_codes.py#L72-L73) which causes the airbrake-python notifier to error [here](https://github.com/airbrake/airbrake-python/blob/master/airbrake/notifier.py#L360) and [here](https://github.com/airbrake/airbrake-python/blob/master/airbrake/notifier.py#L389)

```
HTTPError: 420 Client Error: status code 420 for url: https://airbrake.io/api/v3/projects/PROJECT_ID/notices?key=API_KEY
Logged from file test.py, line 12
```

We should handle this on the client side.

issue_title: [RFC] Separate Protocol (Specs) Enforcement from Formatting
issue_body:
affects #549

### What is wrong?

After reviewing some code within `middleware`, it looks like there is currently no dedicated protocol-enforcement available, most possibly because there was till now only one protocol (specs, yellow-paper) to support.

I have the (gut) feeling that a protocol-verifying code should reside within a separate middleware (like `specs`). The counter-argument is possibly that any further processing results in a compliancy-check. But still, a clean separation would make things cleaner.

### How can it be fixed?

Provide `middleware/compliancy/` or `middleware/specs/ `or `middleware/protocol/` (1), which contains the different middlewares which ensure compliancy to specs like:

- yellow
- poa-geth
- poa-parity
- etc.

Compliancy is loaded before pythonic / formatters. Data-fields are kept compatible to the relevant specification. 

Compliancy should be 'natural', which means that if a user uses web3.py to connect to a chain, web3.py detects the chain-type, and uses the relevant compliancy-middleware.

If this concept hits on implementation-limits, then the protocol-handling can be implemented e.g. directly within a class ChainSpecs and descendants. But it seems that using middleware should be enough.

(1) **protocol**:  'protocol' is used in another context ( 'eth_protocolVersion': '63',). So, this would mean that this term cannot be used.

### Limited Implementation

For the current resolution of #549 (and to avoid introducing to much code-changes) , it would be enough to provide a `/specs/poa_geth` or `/specs/eip225`, which would leave extraData(32) intact (**EXTRA_VANITY**), and move the rest of the data to a 2nd field (**EXTRA_SEAL**, 65 bytes), see ethereum/EIPs/issues/225

.

flake8 v2 supported Python 2.6, and is used by the Travis builds.

flake8 v3 has come out of beta, though is full of bugs, but more importantly it comes with a clear intent to not support Python 2.6, which is the trendy thing to do.
https://gitlab.com/pycqa/flake8/commit/cde783d135953d7d16a1373f13b066731083e477
https://gitlab.com/pycqa/flake8/commit/c8ad626525631314decbb5bd544be47102610fd0

To support continued linting of Python 2.6, raise upstream and/or pin flake8<3 at least on Python 2.6

issue_title: Object leak in logging panel when using threads
issue_body:
I recently tracked down a memory leak in our app, which turned out to be caused by the debug toolbar.

Our Django app launches and destroys a thread in the context of a view (to talk to a websocket server). When that thread writes to logs, debug_toolbar creates a reference to the Thread instance  which it never clears. Disabling DEBUG makes no difference.

Essentially [this call](https://github.com/jazzband/django-debug-toolbar/blob/e7a9ed0c3de1c8635f7e8c83349cb77cef42489f/debug_toolbar/panels/logging.py#L83) only clears the main rendering thread, and not any sub-threads that may have been created in the view.

I think you might want to make `_records` in `LoggingPanel` a weak key dictionary, if I have understood the code correctly...

issue_title: Analysis Module: Best Practices Dumpfile
issue_body:
# Detection issue:
Everything commented `// bad` on (Solidity Recommendations)[https://consensys.github.io/smart-contract-best-practices/recommendations/] page.

## Description: 
Provided in link above.

## Link
^

## Implementation details:Initial implementation ideas/instruction
I'm sure Mythril already catches most or all of the Solidity problems mentioned here. The difference would be in implementation. I recommend autogenerating a dump file that associates best practices excerpts with the associated problem.

So it would look like:

`$ myth calls.sol -x`
Which returns this:
```
==== Message call to external contract ====
Type: Informational
Contract: Caller
Function name: thisisfine()
PC address: 691
This contract executes a message call to to another contract. Make sure that the called contract is trusted and does not execute user-supplied code.
--------------------
In file: solidity_examples/calls.sol:16

fixed_address.call()

--------------------
```
and then gives this message:
```
Total Errors: 1
Errors along with associated best practices have been dumped to calls_sol_analysis.html
```
which then would contain everything under the "External Calls" section (here)[https://consensys.github.io/smart-contract-best-practices/recommendations/] and group similar errors/mistakes together. 

So if `Total Errors:` returned `16` and three of those were "External Calls", then the `myth -x` output for those three would be printed below the associated best practices excerpt(s) pertaining to those three, and then repeated on down the line for the other 13.

To keep the HTML document from getting too cluttered with text, an auto-generated table of contents at the top could link a single heading to each associated group of errors.

>>> ManagementRoot('hei', 'hei', 'hei')

ersion

AttributeError: module 'signal' has no attribute 'SIGALRM'
```

An easy fix would be to just try to explicit import SIGALRM together with signal (f5\bigip\__init__.py):

try:

This would of course disable this functionality on Windows, but I'm thinking that's not the end of the world?

Thanks.

issue_title: ASM Import Policy disallows inline 'file' import
issue_body:
The following code fails:
```
    import_policy = mgmt_root.tm.asm.tasks.import_policy_s.import_policy.create(
        name=name,
        isBase64=True,
        file=policy.decode('utf8')
    )
```
Fails with SDK v2.3.1

The following iContolREST works as expected:
```
(echo '{"isBase64":true, "name": "web-0217_2", "file": "'; base64 web-0217_2.xml; echo '"}') | \
     curl -sku "${F5_USER}:${F5_PASS}" -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d @- \
    https://${F5_SRV}/mgmt/tm/asm/tasks/import-policy
```

Due to `filename` being required in `f5/bigip/tm/asm/tasks.py`:
```
class Import_Policy(AsmResource):
    """BIG-IP® ASM Import Policy Resource."""
    def __init__(self, import_policy_s):
        super(Import_Policy, self).__init__(import_policy_s)
        self._meta_data['required_json_kind'] =\
            'tm:asm:tasks:import-policy:import-policy-taskstate'
        self._meta_data['required_creation_parameters'] = {
            'name', 'filename'}
```
Removing `filename` works.

issue_title: Test markup needed to categorize all tests according to function
issue_body:
We need an effort in our test suites to markup tests with labels that describe the code under test's functionality. I think the best way for now would be to categorize them from a top-down perspective, starting from the most broad description of the suite to the specific type of test. 

The idea is that this would allow a developer to make changes a run a particular suite of tests based on label. I might be working on tests against the '11.6.0' version of BigIP and will naturally want to run those tests as I'm developing.

Note that this doesn't mean that we can only run targeted tests and then call it a day. We still have to make sure all tests pass before being merged in. There's more here to do, but I think starting at the top level might be easiest, rather than labeling one test a 'fuzzy' and having no consensus on what that means.

The definition of done is test labels at least for the following:
- BigIP version
- Module provisioned (asm, afm etc...)
- Feature (clustering)

issue_title: Extend the contents used for DataFrame filenames
issue_body:
This is based on a series of posts first made to Slack. But for the TL;DR:

* The TableFileNameComposer class currently creates filenames that contain only the binning scheme for the DFs
* Sometimes the same binning scheme will be used whilst other variables are changed to create a DF (eg, for systematics, where we change the weighting or corrections).
* I suggest we have TableFileNameComposer.__call__() receive the full DF config specification, such that any generic filename composer one would like to create has the full amount of information available to it.  So on [this line](https://github.com/alphatwirl/alphatwirl/blob/v0.9.x/alphatwirl/configure/TableConfigCompleter.py#L80), you would just pass in `ret`:
```py
ret['outFileName'] = self.createOutFileName(ret)
```

For the meantime, I have a hacky solution to my problem, which can be used instead of TableFileNameComposer:
```py
class WithInsertTableFileNameComposer():                                           
    def __init__(self, composer, inserts):                                         
        self.inserts = inserts                                                     
        self.composer = composer                                                   
        self.frame_idx = 0                                                         
                                                                                   
    def __call__(self, columnNames, indices, **kwargs):                                     
        this_insert = self.inserts[self.frame_idx]                                 
        suffix = kwargs.get("suffix", self.composer.default_suffix)      
        kwargs["suffix"] = "--{}.{}".format(this_insert, suffix)                   
        self.frame_idx += 1                                                        
        return self.composer(columnNames, indices, **kwargs)     
```

issue_title: Package blockstack-core for the Sandstorm app store
issue_body:
We have talked about [Sandstorm](https://github.com/sandstorm-io/sandstorm) in the Blockstack community (I think @shea256 has linked to their blog before) but I don't think we've talked specifically about adding Blockstack to Sandstorm yet. So I am opening this issue to start the discussion. Some background:  

I recently started using Sandstorm to self-host open-source server applications. Self-hosting usually has a relatively high barrier to entry, so relatively few people do it. The result is massive centralization and insecurity of data. Sandstorm is one part of the solution to this problem by making it easy and secure to self-host open-source server applications.  

I would like to see blockstack-core packaged for the Sandstorm app store so Sandstorm users like myself can easily install and run our own Blockstack nodes. You can find instructions for packaging your app for Sandstorm here:  

https://docs.sandstorm.io/en/latest/vagrant-spk/packaging-tutorial/

issue_title: Write coalescing
issue_body:
When sending many small messages (WebSocket and RawSocket), this can lead to significant syscall and CPU load.

Write coalescing buffers up writes to the underlying stream transport (TCP etc) in memory, and then only triggers actual writes every say 200ms.

Here is the idea: https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/compare/master...oberstet:write_coalescing

`txaio` now has `IBatchTimer`, and we could reuse that!

Regarding the value of write coalescing, this is two-fold:

* it reduces the number of syscalls and CPU load (tradeoffs is increased latency vs throughput)
* it reduces the wire level traffic when run over TLS - we should make it such that writes to the underlying Twisted stream protocol only does 1 write

Regarding the latter: http://tavendo.com/blog/post/dissecting-websocket-overhead/

issue_title: Store high-order tensors as 1d vectors
issue_body:
Because catalyst is optimized for 0d and 1d tensors, all the tensors should be stored this way. Of course, users can still input some arrays of arrays at the inputs, but the outputs should be optimized for 1d arrays. It should be the recommended output for anything above 3d tensors.

This can be done only with a more flexible interpretation of the metadata.

One concern is that the data storage as seen by sql may be different from the interpretation seen by tensorframes. On the positive side, it will simplify the low-level operations.

Expected modifications:
 - default storage layout is row major (but with consideration to a potential option to column major)
 - all operations should accept at ingest imbricated arrays or flattened tensors
 - all operations should output flattened tensors for tensors >= 2 dimensions -> this is a user-facing change
 - analyze will be the conversion point between flattened and nested representations, with an extra option `compact_storage`. This option will either accept a single boolean (all numerical types), the letter 'R" (all columns compacted in Row order) or a list of names of columns (only these columns are compacted in Row order). A dictionary could be supported later.
 - printschema will differentiate between tensors stored in 1d and n

issue_title: Job equality broken
issue_body:
There is a test in the legacy suite (*JobCopyConstructor.gpi*) which looks like:
```python
j = Job()
j2 = j.copy()
j3 = Job(j)

assert j == j2
assert j2 == j3
```
Naïvely I expected this to fail as things like the `id` would have changed but I checked the `Job`'s schema and it has `comparable=0` set on the `id`  (and some other items). 

Looking back at previous results I see that this test was failing in 6.0.44, passing in 6.1.14, still passing in 6.1.13 and failing again in 6.1.14.

The code as written should have this test passing as `GangaObject` equivalence ignores attributes with `comparable=0`. The failure for 6.1.16 is due to `postprocessors` not equating which is a bug which needs looking into.

The larger question is *what do we want object equality to look like?* In my opinion two `GangaObject`s should only be equal if *all* their schema attributes are the same. `__eq__` at the moment seems to be performing an "are these two objects a bit similar" test. If we really want this latter comparison then an `is_similar_to()` function might make sense.

Thoughts?

issue_title: GaudiRun support for complex splitting of jobs
issue_body:
I'm still not certain how best to proceed with supporting the workflow of sending unique files per inputfile with each subjob.

I'm assuming that at some level there is the same inputdata being run over (if any) and in my mind this most likely means checking support for IGangaFile objects in the inputfiles within the GenericSplitter.

This has been reported working but it's worth formalising an example JDL and maybe making a unittest around it for this splitter.

At the same time I think it's worth exploring support for ArgSplitter as users may wish to run over the same inputdata with different conditions which are configured through some command-line arguments to their code?
(This is potentially more complex and I'm not sure how to go about supporting this for now, especially in the case of GaudiPython like behaviour where I may have to pass the sys.argc/sys.argv through to the user script which is executed depending how Python internally deals with execfile).


<Aside>
I can imagine a user wanting to change some inputfile and then run a re-processing, but I'm wondering if a user would do something to the extent of submitting many _near-identical_ jobs which may want to be individually run over a large(ish) subset of data.
I'm thinking of some sort of training exercise where a user may wish to run with 3 or 4 different scenarios over maybe ~50LFN in the inputdata which would produce ~150-200 subjobs total.
I'm wondering if there is some nice way to support this in a new type of splitter or not.
i.e. for each inputdata LFN perform multiple subjobs configured as so and collect them all into 1 master job.
aka:
`job.splitter=MyDataGroupSpltter(secondary_splitter=GenericSplitter(someArgs))`
This would pass the master job with all of the options (without inputdata) to the secondary splitter and the returned subjobs are then duplicated such that 1 set of subjobs exists for every LFN in the inputdata is created.
</Aside>

issue_title: 32K (!) files in the gangadir/shared/<user_name>/ directory cause file system error on AFS
issue_body:
AFS has a limit of 64K file names per directory. This limit is reduced depending on the length of the file names, it is reduced to less than 22K if the file names are longer than 33 letters.

https://lists.openafs.org/pipermail/openafs-info/2002-September/005812.html

My `gangadir/shared/koschube/` directory contains `31 707` files currently, and I appear to have hit the limit.

It is no longer possible for me to create new jobs in ganga, I am getting errors like:

```
OSError: [Errno 27] File too large: '/afs/cern.ch/user/k/koschube/workspace/gangadir2/shared/koschube/conf-1e3fe559-6b5e-4db3-a9cb-25912de073ed'`
```

Please note that I had started with a completely new gangadir just a few weeks ago and that I have submitted less than 100 jobs in total (!). So this definitely isn't caused by some outrageous use case.

issue_title: Mesh routing problem
issue_body:
Imagine the following setup: 3 devices A, B and C. A is in reach of B, but out of reach of C and vice-versa. Create network (leader) on node B, then join A and C to B as routers. Using mesh local addresses ping can pass A->B, B->A, B->C, C->B, but not A->C or C->A. The first showstopper I found is when A wants to send a packet to unknown mesh-local adress, it sends address query to B and AddressResolver::HandleAddressQuery is executed, however no response is sent because neither the mesh-C address matches mesh-B (obvious), nor any child of B exists with mesh-C address. To be more precise B->children list is empty. The reason for this is that A and C are removed from children list when A/C->MleRouter::HandleAddressSolicitResponse is called, their short address is chaged from "child-short" to "router-short", LinkRequest sent to B and B->MleRouter::HandleLinkRequest is executed (A and C are removed from B->children when handling Tlv::kSourceAddress).
Without having access to Thread specification I am unsure how this scenario should be handled. Should A and C be left in the B->children list or AddressQuery go past routers or maybe just pass through the AddressQuerry to other routers?
P.S. It seems that multicast addresses are also not retransmitted to other routers/nodes.

issue_title: The `test-changed` goal does not account for the `--tag` filter.
issue_body: Right now [ChangedTargetTask](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/backend/core/tasks/changed_target_goals.py#L17) gets it target selection behavior from the [ChangeCalculator](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/backend/core/tasks/what_changed.py#L17).  Although the `ChangeCalculator` does deal with target address spec filters, it does not deal with the `--tag` filter.  Consider encapsulating all target filtering behavior in a `Subsystem` that the main GoalRunner and the ChangeCalculator can share to prevent drifts in the future.

issue_title: No python 2.6 subprocess.check_output
issue_body:
*setuptools-git-version* fails on python 2.6... I pulled in the 2.7 version of check_output into my `setup.py` like this:

```python
from setuptools import setup, find_packages
import subprocess

if "check_output" not in dir(subprocess):  # duck punch check_output
    from subprocess import Popen, PIPE, CalledProcessError
    STDOUT = subprocess.STDOUT

    def check_output(*popenargs, **kwargs):
        r"""Run command with arguments and return its output as a byte string.

        If the exit code was non-zero it raises a CalledProcessError.  The
        CalledProcessError object will have the return code in the returncode
        attribute and output in the output attribute.

        The arguments are the same as for the Popen constructor.  Example:

        >>> check_output(["ls", "-l", "/dev/null"])
        'crw-rw-rw- 1 root root 1, 3 Oct 18  2007 /dev/null\n'

        The stdout argument is not allowed as it is used internally.
        To capture standard error in the result, use stderr=STDOUT.

        >>> check_output(["/bin/sh", "-c",
        ...               "ls -l non_existent_file ; exit 0"],
        ...              stderr=STDOUT)
        'ls: non_existent_file: No such file or directory\n'
        """
        if 'stdout' in kwargs:
            raise ValueError(
                'stdout argument not allowed, it will be overridden.')
        process = Popen(stdout=PIPE, *popenargs, **kwargs)
        output, unused_err = process.communicate()
        retcode = process.poll()
        if retcode:
            cmd = kwargs.get("args")
            if cmd is None:
                cmd = popenargs[0]

            raise CalledProcessError(retcode, cmd, output=output)
        return output

    subprocess.check_output = check_output

```

Now my builds are happy:
```sh
  py26: commands succeeded
  py27: commands succeeded
  py36: commands succeeded
  congratulations :)
```

issue_title: Bintray account for Streamlink has been rate-limited
issue_body:
> Hi Streamlink,
> 
> Your Bintray account is currently rate-limited due to overuse.
> 
> Customer Support should contact you at the earliest to remove the limits from your account. You may also contact them directly at support@jfrog.com.
> 
> Regards,
> The Bintray Team

I don't know whether the downloads have been abused or not (it doesn't let me see any download statistics on their website), but it looks like we've reached the traffic limit for this month already, and it's just the 9th of February today.

Maybe it's time thinking about a different release model that doesn't force Windows users to download nightlies from an external, auxiliary hoster, just because they want the latest version of a specific plugin. For now, we should add a small note to the download page to tell users to consider downloading a stable release instead of a nightly build, unless they've experienced a bug in the latest version.

As we've already discussed a few months ago (#811), using Github as a hoster for pre-releases could work, but only if we set up a different repo, so that the amount of notifications and spam can be kept down. I don't know though if we would be breaking Github's ToS with that.

Regarding the idea of letting Streamlink update its plugins automatically (#1347), I've already said that I don't think that we should turn Streamlink into a package manager of its own, as there are too many things which need to be taken into account and which also can go wrong. I also doubt that anybody would want to implement this kind of feature here.

My suggestion in order to reduce the number of nightly downloads, is publishing releases more often, in a 14-day schedule or so. This could even be automated, if desired. I don't actively use yt-dl, but they're releasing far more often and are using the [calendar versioning scheme](https://calver.org/#youtube_dl). This lets people immediately know how old a release is and if we'd publish a new release every two weeks, the intention of downloading a nightly build gets reduced greatly.

issue_title: streamlink doesn't run under PyPy
issue_body:
If I try to run streamlink under PyPy, I get this:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/opt/pypy/bin/streamlink", line 11, in <module>
    load_entry_point('streamlink==0.5.0', 'console_scripts', 'streamlink')()
  File "/opt/pypy/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 565, in load_entry_point
    return get_distribution(dist).load_entry_point(group, name)
  File "/opt/pypy/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 2631, in load_entry_point
    return ep.load()
  File "/opt/pypy/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 2291, in load
    return self.resolve()
  File "/opt/pypy/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 2297, in resolve
    module = __import__(self.module_name, fromlist=['__name__'], level=0)
  File "/opt/pypy/site-packages/streamlink_cli/main.py", line 17, in <module>
    from streamlink.stream import StreamProcess
  File "/opt/pypy/site-packages/streamlink/stream/__init__.py", line 4, in <module>
    from streamlink.stream.akamaihd import AkamaiHDStream
  File "/opt/pypy/site-packages/streamlink/stream/akamaihd.py", line 15, in <module>
    from ..packages.flashmedia import FLV, FLVError
  File "/opt/pypy/site-packages/streamlink/packages/flashmedia/__init__.py", line 4, in <module>
    from .amf import *
  File "/opt/pypy/site-packages/streamlink/packages/flashmedia/amf.py", line 3, in <module>
    from .types import AMF0String, AMF0Value, U8, U16BE, U32BE
  File "/opt/pypy/site-packages/streamlink/packages/flashmedia/types.py", line 298, in <module>
    U24BE = HighLowCombo(">HB", 8, True)
TypeError: __new__() takes exactly 2 arguments (4 given)
```

The cause of this is that the `Struct` base class for `PrimitiveType` has a `__new__()` function with different arguments:
```
$ pypy -c "import struct; help(struct.Struct.__new__)"
Help on function __new__:

__new__(subtype, format)

$ python2 -c "import struct; help(struct.Struct.__new__)"
Help on built-in function __new__:

__new__(...)
    T.__new__(S, ...) -> a new object with type S, a subtype of T
```

This smells like it could be a PyPy bug, so I've raised this as an issue in the PyPy project to see what they have to say about it:

https://bitbucket.org/pypy/pypy/issues/2551/structstruct-has-weird-__new__-arguments

But in the meantime... perhaps the better approach is to simply not inherit from Struct to begin with:
```diff
diff --git a/src/streamlink/packages/flashmedia/types.py b/src/streamlink/packages/flashmedia/types.py
index cc4fcca..ee5f3d2 100644
--- a/src/streamlink/packages/flashmedia/types.py
+++ b/src/streamlink/packages/flashmedia/types.py
@@ -29,10 +29,17 @@ AMF3_MIN_INTEGER = -268435456
 AMF3_MAX_INTEGER = 268435455
 
 
-class PrimitiveType(Struct):
+class PrimitiveType(object):
     def __call__(self, *args):
         return self.pack(*args)
 
+    def __init__(self, strformat):
+        self.struct = Struct(strformat)
+
+    @property
+    def size(self):
+        return self.struct.size
+
     def read(self, fd):
         data = fd.read(self.size)
 
@@ -41,6 +48,18 @@ class PrimitiveType(Struct):
 
         return self.unpack(data)[0]
 
+    def pack(self, *args):
+        return self.struct.pack(*args)
+
+    def pack_into(self, *args):
+        return self.struct.pack_into(*args)
+
+    def unpack(self, *args):
+        return self.struct.unpack(*args)
+
+    def unpack_from(self, *args):
+        return self.struct.unpack_from(*args)
+
 
 class PrimitiveClassType(PrimitiveType):
     def __init__(self, format, cls):
```

This should work for both CPython and PyPy, and is probably the cleaner fix. I don't know what (if any) other `Struct` member functions need to be wrapped if this code path actually gets hit though. I don't think I'm hitting it with my test case of streaming from Twitch.tv.

Thoughts?

issue_title: Graphs with long chains of conditionals are suboptimally laid out.
issue_body:
Graphs with long chains of `cond` ops are presented suboptimally in the graph explorer - sometimes, to the extent of not being usable. For instance, this graph here is barely visible at the default zoom level.

![long_long](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4221553/29589768-62a1f02c-874b-11e7-9c25-77f546a0afe4.png)

One has to strategically zoom in to view the graph:

![long_long](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4221553/29589831-c2b9f3f6-874b-11e7-8fa5-3a7e2fa4a910.png)

and several have noted that doing so is hard. We could consider grouping the conditional node into some group node similar to a series node.

issue_title: Image issue (totally blank) of "Download PNG" with tensorboard 1.5.0.
issue_body:
## Issue description

Image generated on "Download PNG" is totally blank with tensorboard 1.5.0.

Image generated on "Download PNG" is fine after replaced tensorboard 1.5.0 with tensorboard 0.4.0.

## Environment

- PC 1 (GPU version): Windows 10 + Python 3.6.3 + TensorFlow-GPU 1.5.0 + Tensorboard 1.5.0 + CUDA 9.0 + cuDNN 7.0.5 
- PC 2 (CPU version): Windows 7 + Python 3.6.3 + TensorFlow 1.5.0 + Tensorboard 1.5.0
- PC 3 (CPU version): Windows 7 + Python 3.6.3 + TensorFlow 1.5.0 + Tensorboard 1.5.0

> Not tested on Linux yet.

## Issue Steps

1. Install python 3.6.3, tensorflow 1.5.0 (GPU version or CPU version).

> Dependency package tensorboard 1.5.0 will be installed with tensorflow 1.5.0.

2. Generate a log, such as the code below:

```
import tensorflow as tf

sess = tf.InteractiveSession()

with tf.name_scope('a'):
  a = tf.Variable(4, dtype=tf.int32)
with tf.name_scope('b'):
  b = tf.Variable(3, dtype=tf.int32)
with tf.name_scope('c'):
  c = tf.multiply(a, b)
tf.summary.scalar('output', c)

merged = tf.summary.merge_all()
summary, c_val = sess.run([merged, c], feed_dict={a: 4, b: 5})
writer = tf.summary.FileWriter(r"C:\tmp", graph=sess.graph)
writer.add_summary(summary)
writer.close()
```

3. Run `tensorboard --logdir=C:\tmp`, then open tensorboard (port 6006) in Chrome browser.

4. Click `Download PNG` on the left sidebar of `TensorBoard` - `GRAPHS`.

- File name: `graph_large_attrs_key=_too_large_attrs&limit_attr_size=1024&run=.png`
- File size: `3.06 KB`
- File pixel: 495 * 286

5. Open the image, but the image shows nothing invalid. The image is totally blank.

## Recovery Steps

1. Download "tensorflow_tensorboard-0.4.0-py3-none-any.whl" on [PYPI](https://testpypi.python.org/pypi/tensorflow-tensorboard/0.4.0)

2. `pip3 install tensorflow_tensorboard-0.4.0-py3-none-any.whl`. After installation tensorboard 0.4.0, the tensorflow version is still 1.5.0.

3. **Use the same log in Issue Step 2**. Run `tensorboard --logdir=C:\tmp`, then open tensorboard (port 6006) in Chrome browser.

4. Click `Download PNG` on the left sidebar of `TensorBoard` - `GRAPHS`.

- File name: `graph_large_attrs_key=_too_large_attrs&limit_attr_size=1024&run=D%5C.png`
- File size: `11.9 KB`
- File pixel: `495 * 286`

5. Open the image, the image shows graph successfully.

issue_title: Icon Request: Converge most Form Control Icon to the same general size.
issue_body:
I'm using [this nifty CSS trick](http://codepen.io/jamesbarnett/pen/yILjk/) to customize a few checkbox icons.
For my design the combination of checked/unchecked icons that feel more natural is [fa-square-o](http://fortawesome.github.io/Font-Awesome/icon/square-o/) and [fa-check-square](http://fortawesome.github.io/Font-Awesome/icon/check-square/); the problem is when clicked, as the width is slightly off you can see the label shift.

The CSS trick actually already has a small margin we can use for similar situations but I think the problem might be in the sub pixel scale so while removing 1px on the letter-spacing from 10px to 9px helps it doesn't fully correct the effect.

It would be awesome if you guys could redesign them to give more freedom to recombine icons.

Regarding [fa-check-square-o](http://fortawesome.github.io/Font-Awesome/icon/check-square-o/) there isn't much to do and while I don't care much about it at the moment if the width delta to the others was a natural number it would be easy to hide by adjusting the letter-spacing.

Let me know if you guys need anything.

issue_title: Compilation and letsencrypt-auto
issue_body:
Suppose the following sequence of events:

1. You run `letsencrypt-auto` to completion.
2. You run `apt-get upgrade -y`. Further suppose an upgrade for a package like OpenSSL was available.
3. You run `letsencrypt-auto`.

The version of OpenSSL we complied `python-cryptography` against has changed. Couldn't this break your `venv` until it is recreated? If so, perhaps we should do something to prevent this situation.

issue_title: Manual certonly request fails due to newlines in .well-known string
issue_body:
tl;dr: Self-verify of challenge from HTTP server fails; algorithm needs to chomp/trim newlines present in HTTP response.

I am participating in the closed beta and I requested my first cert yesterday.  I am terminating SSL on an external provider so I used the following invocation for letsencrypt-auto:

./letsencrypt-auto -a manual certonly

This process requested that I place a random token at a URL path under /.well-known/acme-challenge/ .  I did so, and tested it with curl, but it still failed to validate.  The error I got was "Self-verify of challenge failed, authorization abandoned."  I ran it again with verbose logging, and noticed this in the log:

2015-11-09 19:11:02,800:DEBUG:acme.challenges:Key authorization from response (u'X6K-MQsdzpMUVNNGoyboRVIxmvfTnNEPbwIoo6Cy2ZU.1WvRR9HsnS_PQRS4VJgCE_gMf8Y28ELCVGXCPlbqVNA
') doesn't match HTTP response (u'X6K-MQsdzpMUVNNGoyboRVIxmvfTnNEPbwIoo6Cy2ZU.1WvRR9HsnS_PQRS4VJgCE_gMf8Y28ELCVGXCPlbqVNA\n\n')

Note the strings are only different because of the newlines.  I generated the file the first time using vi, which added a newline for me.  I had to use "echo -n" to generate the file instead, which wrote the string with no newline.  Many people will embed a newline when writing this file and I think the Let's Encrypt client should be modified to tolerate it.

issue_title: A suspected bug when use multi-GPU across nodes
issue_body:
I'm not sure if it's bug of my code or ray. 
The phenomenon is strange that when use multi-GPU across nodes, it just has a error of segmentation fault (core dumped). When the used GPUs do not come from different nodes, the code works ! (for example, set the number of actor to 4.  We have two nodes, and each node has four GPU)

Here is the error log. Yes, it is very short
```
**_Segmentation fault (core dumped)_**
```

Here is my code (I have removed the irrelevant part)
Firstly, let us define an actor.
```
import ray
ray.init(redis_address="9.91.9.123:6379")
class test(object):
    def __init__(self, model_config):
        import os

        os.environ['CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES'] = ','.join([str(i) for i in ray.get_gpu_ids()])
        from keras.models import model_from_config
        self.model = model_from_config(model_config, custom_objects={})
        self.weights = None
    def run(self):
        for i in range(1000):
            self.model.predict(np.zeros((100,1,4)))
        return 1

    def get_weights(self):
        import numpy as np
        return [np.zeros((2,2)),np.zeros((2,2))]

    def set_weights(self, weights):
        self.weights = weights
```
Then i define the model
```
import numpy as np
import gym
import ray
from keras.models import Sequential
from keras.layers import Dense, Activation, Flatten

model = Sequential()
model.add(Flatten(input_shape=(1,) + (4,)))
model.add(Dense(16))
model.add(Activation('relu'))
model.add(Dense(16))
model.add(Activation('relu'))
model.add(Dense(16))
model.add(Activation('relu'))
model.add(Dense(2))
model.add(Activation('linear'))
```
then i start to train on two nodes, and each node has four GPUs.
The following code looks a bit complicated at first glance. In fact, 
it is a simple averaging operation between multiple agents' return weights
```
config = {
    'class_name': model.__class__.__name__,
    'config': model.get_config(),
}

agent_count = 8

cpu_actor = ray.actor(num_gpus=1)(test)
agents =[cpu_actor(config) for _ in range(agent_count)]
rollout = 20
for k in range(rollout):
    rewards=[]
    actions = []
    weights=[]
    for i in range(agent_count):
        actions.append(agents[i].run())
    for i in range(agent_count):
        id,actions = ray.wait(actions)
        rewards.append(ray.get(id))
    for i in range(agent_count):
        weights.append(ray.get(agents[i].get_weights()))
    newweights=[]
    for i in range(len(weights[0])):
        temp = np.zeros(weights[0][i].shape)
        for j in range(agent_count):
            temp += weights[j][i]/agent_count
        newweights.append(temp)
    idcol = []
    for i in range(agent_count):
        idcol.append(agents[i].set_weights(newweights))
    for i in range(agent_count):
        ray.wait(idcol)
    print("step {},reward {}".format(k,np.mean(rewards)))
```

import ray

        for key in obj:

def calculate_sum_distributed(obj_list, num_ray_tasks=10):

            end_idx = len(obj_list)
        else:
            end_idx = start_idx + idx_delta
        
        task_id = calculate_sum.remote(obj_list_id, start_idx, end_idx)
        ray_tasks.append(task_id)

    return sum(ray.get(ray_tasks))

def get_random_obj_list():

for i in range(0, 1000):

issue_title: multipart/mixed request support
issue_body:
`multipart/mixed` support needed.

https://medium.com/ringcentral-developers/updating-ringcentral-user-extension-greetings-using-the-rest-api-and-ruby-db325022c6ee

POST `/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/greeting`  only supports `multipart/mixed`. So there is no way to do the same thing mentioned in the blog article with this SDK.

Fax sending and MMS sending do support `multipart/form-data`. But there are still endpoints which requires `multipart/mixed`.

I am going to submit a PR soon.

issue_title: Add Support for CJK Languages
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm working on adding support for parsing CJK (Chinese, Japanese, Korean) languages and had a few questions.

I have defined a `zh_parserinfo` class and have been able to get a test case like this to work:

    def test_zh_dates(self):
        date = DateParser(language='zh').parse(u'2014年10月4日', date_format='%Y年%m月%d日')   
        self.assertEqual(date.year, 2014)
        self.assertEqual(date.month, 10)
        self.assertEqual(date.day, 4)

However, this is not ideal. Dates are always written year-month-day, so it would be nice if `parse` handled it by default, and the above test case would pass without specifying this `date_format`. There are also two cases to support:

    # 年 means year, 月 means month, 日 means day
    # Dates are always written year first.
    date = DateParser(language='zh').parse(u'2014年10月4日')   
    
    # This is the same date, but it is written with Chinese numbers. 
    # 二 = 2, 〇 = 0, 一 = 1, 四 =  4, 十 = 10, etc...
    date = DateParser(language='zh').parse(u'二〇一四年十月四日')  # formal usage

Where would you suggest putting the code to handle the mapping of Chinese numbers to standard numbers? Any another suggestions for implementing this are appreciated. Thanks!
  
Edit: I'm not familiar with Arabic, but in #6 it looks like a similar mapping is needed. Something generic that could work for CJK languages, Arabic, and whatever other languages might need this would be best.

issue_title: Incompatible with django-autocomplete-light
issue_body:
This package works wonder (thanks) by itself but it does things differently from the default django admin site, it becomes a bit of a pain when paired with [django-autocomplete-light](https://github.com/yourlabs/django-autocomplete-light).

Note that in addition of the issues below, yourlabs/django-autocomplete-light/ will need to be fixed too in order to achieve full compatibility.

### Issue 1: Inserting the row before renaming the attributes

Django [updates the attributes](https://github.com/django/django/blob/94cd8efc50c717cd00244f4b2233f971a53b205e/django/contrib/admin/static/admin/js/inlines.js#L82) before [inserting the row](https://github.com/django/django/blob/94cd8efc50c717cd00244f4b2233f971a53b205e/django/contrib/admin/static/admin/js/inlines.js#L85) but nested admin [inserts the row](https://github.com/theatlantic/django-nested-admin/blob/fdccdce079fc2f4f48dabf950e6fb9c90d488152/nested_admin/static/nested_admin/src/nested-admin/jquery.djangoformset.js#L245) before [updating the attributes](https://github.com/theatlantic/django-nested-admin/blob/fdccdce079fc2f4f48dabf950e6fb9c90d488152/nested_admin/static/nested_admin/src/nested-admin/jquery.djangoformset.js#L252-L254) which means when initializing an item on [DOMNodeInserted](https://github.com/yourlabs/django-autocomplete-light/blob/622b10757bbd0117c6e4b93e6afc68e124e6b764/src/dal/static/autocomplete_light/autocomplete.init.js#L53-L55), it will fail because not all the element is ready.

FWIW, I don't think it would be possible to initialise on the `formset:added` event because this is supposed to work for regular field for which `formset:added` will never be called.

### Issue 2: Not updating the attributes on all elements

Nested admin only replace the attributes on a [subset of elements](https://github.com/theatlantic/django-nested-admin/blob/1dba49177e32f2fddd35f191cc836bd88114ac49/nested_admin/static/nested_admin/src/nested-admin/utils.js#L22) while Django replace the attributes on [every elements](https://github.com/django/django/blob/94cd8efc50c717cd00244f4b2233f971a53b205e/django/contrib/admin/static/admin/js/inlines.js#L81-L82). However autocomplete light [uses a div](https://github.com/yourlabs/django-autocomplete-light/blob/08d69f0938836d511e1866f77286237c6e4f897b/src/dal/widgets.py#L90-L92) for its field configuration and it doesn't get processed.

issue_title: [V3 Streams/Core] Use of aiohttp.ClientSession assignment
issue_labels: [{'id': 593836458, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/Cog-Creators/Red-DiscordBot/labels/V3', 'name': 'V3', 'color': '006b75', 'default': False}, {'id': 781440756, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/Cog-Creators/Red-DiscordBot/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': '0e8a16', 'default': True}, {'id': 652936203, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/Cog-Creators/Red-DiscordBot/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': '0e8a16', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
### Type:

- Bug

### Brief description of the problem
In various places throughout the bot (namely in streams and core_commands), `aiohttp.ClientSession()` is used like this:
```py
session = aiohttp.ClientSession()
async with session.request(url) as r:
    # Do stuff with response
await session.close()
```

The session should be used with an async context manager, so that it closes successfully even if an error is raised, or the function returns, whilst it is being used:
```py
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
    async with session.request(url) as r:
        # Do stuff with response
```

issue_title: Export TPOT pipelines to Orange file format
issue_body:
[Orange](http://orange.biolab.si/) has a pretty nice interface for building sklearn pipelines in a GUI.

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1719223/11642314/4e581870-9d0b-11e5-84eb-d50f2a023a3f.png)

It'd be great to have a function that exports TPOT pipelines into a file format that can be opened with Orange.

I've contacted the Orange devs and they said that it should be relatively straightforward to accomplish this _as long as Orange has each TPOT pipeline operator already implemented as an Orange widget_.

I'd imagine a good first step would be to thoroughly explore the Orange software and see what widgets/pipeline operators they already have implemented.

---

Here's an example Orange file:

issue_title: Connection still up after 'state -> down' notification
issue_body:
Hi Thomas,

We found a peer which triggered the following log message:
```
Oct  3 08:57:51 rrc19 exabgp: 4673   reactor       New peer setup: neighbor 196.46.25.216 local-ip 196.46.25.86 local-as 12654 peer-as 2018 router-id 196.46.25.86 family-allowed in-open
Oct  3 08:57:51 rrc19 exabgp: 4673   reactor       New peer setup: neighbor 2001:43f8:6d0::216 local-ip 2001:43f8:6d0::86 local-as 12654 peer-as 2018 router-id 196.46.25.86 family-allowed in-open
Oct  3 08:57:54 rrc19 exabgp: 4673   network       Connected to peer neighbor 196.46.25.216 local-ip 196.46.25.86 local-as 12654 peer-as 2018 router-id 196.46.25.86 family-allowed in-open (out)
Oct  3 08:57:54 rrc19 exabgp: 4673   network       Connected to peer neighbor 2001:43f8:6d0::216 local-ip 2001:43f8:6d0::86 local-as 12654 peer-as 2018 router-id 196.46.25.86 family-allowed in-open (out)
Oct  4 00:48:47 rrc19 exabgp: 4673   network       Peer   196.46.25.216 ASN 2018    automatic protocol cleanup
Oct  4 00:48:47 rrc19 exabgp: 4673   network       Peer 2001:43f8:6d0::216 ASN 2018    automatic protocol cleanup
```
whatever this event at Oct  4 00:48:47 was, it sent the following to client process:
```
{
  "neighbor": {
    "reason": "automatic protocol cleanup",
    "state": "down",
    "asn": {
      "peer": "2018",
      "local": "12654"
    },
    "address": {
      "peer": "196.46.25.216",
      "local": "196.46.25.86"
    },
    "ip": "196.46.25.216"
  },
  "type": "state",
  "counter": 3837054,
  "ppid": "1",
  "pid": "4673",
  "host": "rrc19.ripe.net",
  "time": 1475542127.33,
  "exabgp": "3.4.8"
}

{
  "neighbor": {
    "reason": "automatic protocol cleanup",
    "state": "down",
    "asn": {
      "peer": "2018",
      "local": "12654"
    },
    "address": {
      "peer": "2001:43f8:6d0::216",
      "local": "2001:43f8:6d0::86"
    },
    "ip": "2001:43f8:6d0::216"
  },
  "type": "state",
  "counter": 8379281,
  "ppid": "1",
  "pid": "4673",
  "host": "rrc19.ripe.net",
  "time": 1475542127.33,
  "exabgp": "3.4.8"
}
```
But the connection stayed up, and we continue to receive update messages from the peer, these are still being passed onto the client process.

This confused our RIB state processing engine as the last 'state' change for the peer was 'down'.

Do you have any idea how this might have happened? It looks like this originates from Protocol.close, but that looks like it should kill the connection, so we don't understand how the connection is still up :)
(v.3.4.15)

Thanks!
Colin

When does Producer.flush() return?
issue_body:
I am running:

confluent_kafka.version ('0.9.2', 590080), confluent_kafka.libversion ('0.9.1', 590335)

I have a Producer, and I have a loop wherein I send a single short message followed immediately by a flush(). Since I have not explicitly set batch.num.messages or queue.buffering.max.ms in the config I handed in when I created the Producer, I assume these take their default values (10000 messages and 1000 ms, respectively). That is, if I send a single message, I would assume that it would sit on a queue for 1000 ms (one second), then get sent. Since flush() will block until Producer.len() is 0, I would expect flush to block for this amount of time (one second). In my production code, this is in fact what I see. In a test script, however, I see that flush() returns after about exactly 0.1 seconds, i.e. 100 ms. My config in this situation looks like this (Note that some things are commented out):

    conf = {'bootstrap.servers': kafka_broker,

I see that Producer_flush calls Producer_poll0(500) calls rd_kafka_poll(tmout (i.e. 500)) calls rd_kafka_q_serve(timeout_ms) but then things get confusing to me. issue_title: Decode percent-encoding in mixed text
issue_body:
Right now, `_url._percent_decode()` has a fast and silent path with some surprising results. You can pass in text with percent encoding present, and get that text back out, unmodified, if there are any non-ASCII characters present.

```python
>>> _percent_decode(u'é%3Dmc^2')
u'é%3Dmc^2'
```

This poses an obvious problem to decoding IRI values containing reserved characters, as is the case for DecodedURL. #54 worked around this by re-percent-encoding everything before percent decoding it. Aside from being a bit hacky, there are more efficient ways of approaching this.

issue_title: Compiler generates invalid code for ANSI C
global imports
issue_body:
I know that dill can have issues pickling global variables, but I was wondering if the following case is intended. My use case for this is setting up a job on a given machine, pickling the instructions, sending it over to remote machines, unpickling the objects, and running the jobs there.

Run the following in one file.

```
import dill
import numpy as np

def test():
  return np

if __name__ == "__main__":
  with open("out", "w") as f:
    f.write(dill.dumps(test))
```

Run the next in a different file. Running in a different file is necessary to emulate the environment I am using, where the numpy import is not included at the target machine.

```
import dill

with open("out") as f:
  contents = f.read()

res = dill.loads(contents)

print(res())
```

This yields:
```
NameError: global name 'np' is not defined
```

However, the code works if, instead of the first file, I define the function ```test``` in a separate file (so that it is in a formal namespace, not "__main__", load and pickle it from a separate runner, the numpy import also gets packaged and unpickled successfully.

In other words, if we have a file

```
import numpy as np

def test():
  return np
```

and we have a separate file

```
from source import test

with open("out", "w") as f:
  f.write(dill.dumps(test))
```

then the unpickling is fine.

As it turns out, setting ```dill.settings["recurse"] = True``` fixes this particular example, but in my actual script, which is more complicated, doing so raises:

```
RuntimeError: maximum recursion depth exceeded in cmp
```

Can I get global imports such that:
1) I do not have to define a separate file for the function I am pickling
2) I do not trigger maximum recursion depth?

issue_title: It is not always relevant to silently ignore unrecognized properties
issue_body:
Consider the following code:
```python
    CTX={ "foo": "http://example.com/foo"}
    expanded = jsonld.expand({"fooo":"bar"}, {'expandContext': CTX})
```
The result is an empty list, because "fooo" is not recognized by CTX. The fact that it is *silently* ignore can be relevant in some situations, but in not in others. In this example, it is probably a typo, which the user would probably like to be detected.

So I think there should be a way to be notified when some JSON attributes were ignored.

The simplest (but not flexible) way to do it would be to have a ``strict`` option, defaulting to False. When set to True, an exception would be raised whenever a JSON key could not be converted to a IRI. In my application, I would rather fail than silently drop some data submitted by the user.

A more flexible solution would be to have a ``droppedKeys`` option, expecting a list, and populating this list with every key that could not be converted to an IRI.
```python
    CTX={ "foo": "http://example.com/foo"}
    dropped = []
    expanded = jsonld.expand({"fooo":"bar"}, {'expandContext': CTX, 'droppedKeys': dropped})
    if dropped:
        # print some warning message to the user
    # but still process expended
```

issue_title: "found" field added after MyDocType.get(): expected behaviour?
issue_body:
I've been playing around with the persistance example.
I just noticed that after I use the get method, there is a "found" field added to my document.
Saving, then adds that field to the document, and obviously to the mapping.

Is this the expected behaviour?

see https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/pull/1752 and https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/pull/1636

        options=channel_args)

    health_stub = health_pb2_grpc.HealthStub(channel)

issue_title: Special handling of `path` in `gitlab.mixins.ListMixin.list` cripples endpoints with `path` query parameters
issue_body:
Pulling the `path` key from `kwargs` makes it impossible or at least very hard/ugly to call endpoints that support a `path` query parameter, like with the commits endpoint. Even though the docs do not say anything about the parameter it was added quite a while ago https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab-ce/blob/146d4348ca6812e26729de40a831f4ae8c27fde6/lib/api/commits.rb and is still available on the master branch https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab-ce/blob/master/lib/api/commits.rb

At least to me after a quick look around, there seems no easy & clean way to fix this without breaking compatibility.

There is another case (`gitlab.Gitlab._raw_list`) where this is handled with a positional argument (`path_`).

issue_title: Add support to look up merge requests associated with a commit
issue_body:
## Description of the problem, including code/CLI snippet
Starting in GitLab 10.7, the API now supports retrieving merge requests associated with a commit. 


## Expected Behavior
Ideally, we'd be able to use `python-gitlab` to look up merge requests associated with each commit.

## Actual Behavior
As far as I can tell, this new feature hasn't yet been implemented in Python-Gitlab.


## Specifications

  - python-gitlab version: 1.4.0
  - API version you are using (v3/v4): v4
  - Gitlab server version (or gitlab.com): 10.8.2-ee

issue_title: Don't use "traceback2" for CPython >= 3.5
issue_body:
traceback2 is a backport of the traceback module of python 3.5 so there is no need for it in Python >= 3.5. Please change the requirements file and the import to conditionally require unittest2 only for older Python version.

Each additional package makes distribution maintainers life more complicated, since they have to maintain yet another package even if the distribution only includes Python 3.5 or even 3.6.

Thanks.

issue_title: How to handle camera disconnection while streaming?
issue_body:
| Required Info  |   |
|---|---|
| Camera Model |  SR300 | 
| Firmware Version |  3.17.0.0 | 
| Operating System & Version |  Windows 10  |
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)  |   |
| SDK Version | 2.8.3 |

I am writing my own interface on top of librealsense to handle the camera (only one camera active at any given time).

Everything seem to work like a charm, i receive the events when a camera connects or disconnects, i can start and stop a camera to send the depth frames.

The problems arise when I disconnect the camera while streaming. 
After that if I try to reconnect the same camera, the connection event is not fired but, if I connect and disconnect another camera they are fired as always.

To handle the connection/disconnection event I use:
    
     rs2::contenxt::set_devices_changed_callback

To enable the device I want to use I rely on:

    rs2::config config;
    config.enable_stream(RS2_STREAM_DEPTH);
    config.enable_device("someserialnumber");

To start streaming I use:

     rs2::pipeline pipe;
     pipe.start(config);

To generate the frames i use:

    rs2::frameset data = pipe.wait_for_frames(); 
    rs2::frame depth = data.get_depth_frame(); 

When I disconnect the camera:
1) I receive the disconnect signal
2) the programs is blocked for a few seconds waiting for new frames
3) the programs throws a rs2::camera_disconnected_error

What should I do when I receive the disconnection signal? 
Should I have to turn off the pipeline, the device, the sensor or whatever ?

issue_body:

| Camera Model | SR300 | 
| Firmware Version | 3.21.0.0  | 
| Operating System & Version | Windows 10 |
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)  |   |

Does anyone else have this problem?  The 2D PNG files written by the Intel RealSense Viewer (v2.8.1) appear to have an incorrect format. The cam works fine for me and both 2D and 3D images look correct in the viewer. The 3D .PLY files also appear correct. But I cannot get the PNG files generated (sample attached) to open correctly in any program. ImageJ claims that they are RGB images with all R/G/B values being equal and values ranging only from 228-255 (i.e. 8-bit and of limited range). A low-level PNG viewer such as TweakPNG says that the image has 8 bits per sample with grayscale+alpha data. Gimp2 also thinks there's an alpha layer. Nothing I've tried loads a 16-bit grayscale image, as expected. Using TweakPNG I edited the PNG image header forcing the data to load as a 16-bit grayscale image, but the image is still grossly incorrect (values along what is a flat surface swing from 1,000-60,000).

I am a user and a rusty programmer, and have not looked at the source code for the Viewer.

issue_title: Stop buffering stdout/stderr
issue_body:
When used in scripts that do unbuffered logging (e.g. via the python `logging` module), captured output does not follow a logical flow.

My long-term suggestion would be to stop using `print` for most applications and switch to using the `logging` module.

issue_title: RFE: Available functions should be discoverable via CLI option flag
issue_body:
Currently, running `fissfc -h` gives:
```
usage: fissfc [-h] [-u API_URL] [-v] [-V] [-y]

FISS: The FireCloud CLI

optional arguments:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
  -u API_URL, --url API_URL
                        Firecloud api url. Your default is
                        https://api.firecloud.org/api
  -v, --version         show program's version number and exit
  -V, --verbose         Turn on verbosity (e.g. show URL of REST calls)
  -y, --yes             Assume yes for any prompts
```

Currently, it seems that the only way to see the available functions is by giving `fissfc` an invalid argument:
```
% fissfc hello
usage: fissfc [-h] [-u API_URL] [-v] [-V] [-y]
fissfc: error: invalid choice: 'hello' (choose from 'space_new', 'space_exists', 'space_delete', 'space_info', 'space_list', 'space_lock', 'space_unlock', 'space_clone', 'entity_import', 'entity_types', 'entity_list', 'participant_list', 'sample_list', 'sset_list', 'entity_delete', 'participant_delete', 'sample_delete', 'sset_delete', 'space_acl', 'space_set_acl', 'flow_new', 'flow_delete', 'flow_acl', 'flow_set_acl', 'flow_list', 'config_list', 'config_get', 'config_copy', 'config_acl', 'ping', 'attr_get', 'attr_set', 'attr_copy', 'attr_delete', 'attr_fill_null', 'mop', 'noop', 'config', 'flow_start', 'sset_loop', 'monitor', 'supervise', 'supervise_recover', 'space_search', 'entity_copy', 'proj_list', 'config_validate', 'runnable')
```

Once at this point, an intrepid user can try using `-h` with one of the valid functions to get usage, but there is no documentation of this:
```
% fissfc space_new -h
usage: fissfc space_new [-h] [-w WORKSPACE] [-p PROJECT]
                        [--authdomain AUTHDOMAIN]

Create new workspace

                        Workspace name

                        Limit access to the workspace to a specific
